name: Optics and Photonics Tulane Innovation Club and Society
description:
The Optics and Photonics Tulane Innovation Club and Society (OPTICS) is the Tulane student affiliate chapter of the OPTICA (formerly OSA) Society. This organization is aligned with OPTICA’s commitment to promote the science of light and its interdisciplinary applications. Tulane’s chapter will equip students with the available benefits that OPTICA provides, including travel grants for national conferences, lectures from experts in the field, mentorship and networking opportunities, and student leadership experiences in annual technical conferences. OPTICS will raise awareness of the potential for new light-based technologies and promote enthusiastic research efforts in building an inclusive and impactful future with optical science.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Optics and Photonics Tulane Innovation Club and Society is dedicated to spreading knowledge of fundamental optical science and cutting-edge research in the field of photonics.

name: Public Relations Student Society of America Tulane University Chapter
description:
PRSSA, the foremost organization for students interested in the public relations and communications fields, announced the establishment of a Chapter at Tulane University. This marks the 11th school in Louisiana to be affiliated with PRSSA’s nearly 375 Chapters worldwide, continuing the organization’s mission to build a robust, widespread network of students at institutions of higher learning. 


Students can contact prssa@tulane.edu for information on membership and visit https://www.prsa.org/prssa/chapter-firm-resources/find-a-prssa-chapter to learn more. 

At this time, TU PRSSA does not have any chapter dues, so students would only be responsible for the national membership dues ($55 at this time). 
 
While the TU PRSSA Chapter is housed in the Tulane School of Professional Advancement, all Tulane students are eligible to join!

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: PRSSA is the foremost organization for students interested in public relations and communications. Founded in 1968 by its parent organization, the PRSA, PRSSA includes nearly 7,000 student members and advisers on nearly 375 campuses.

name: Tulane Brain Institute
summary: Neuroscience undergrad and grad programs.

name: Violence Prevention Institute
description:
As part of its mission to foster transformative research, training, and advocacy around all forms of violence, VPI offers learning and networking opportunities for faculty, staff, students, alumni, and the public. 

 

The VPI is housed in the Tulane University School of Public Health and Tropical Medicine, Department of Social, Behavioral, and Population Sciences (SBPS), and supports faculty and students from all Tulane schools.
summary: The Violence Prevention Institute (VPI) at Tulane University is an equity focused hub supporting communities to foster transformative research, training, and advocacy to address systemic, structural, and interpersonal violence.

name: Mentor Institute

summary: The Mentor Institute is the centralized hub for all campus mentorship programs.

name: Wave of Support
description:
Caring for your own and other’s wellbeing is multifaceted. We are here to help. 

Wave of Support is a campus-wide, collaborative program that supports students’ mental and emotional health at Tulane University. Wave of Support brings together the programs and services of the many existing student support offices on campus with the goal of building a healthier campus by enhancing the holistic experience of our students in the realm of wellbeing.

 

Wave of Support offers a variety of programming and events that support the overall mental wellness of our Tulane community.

-The Wave of Support Leadership Academy brings together a cohort of first-year students to explore the connections between our leadership practice and the ways we care for ourselves, each other, and our communities. 

-The Mentor Institute provides a strong foundation for our student mentors through a universal training curriculum and centralized resources

-Tulane Active Minds is the nation’s premier nonprofit organization supporting mental health awareness and education for young adults.

Wave of Support is also available to host workshops for classes, organizations and groups of students, faculty, and staff. We now also offer QPR (Question, Persuade, Refer) training and debrief options. Follow us @tulanewaveofsupport for even more information on upcoming events and programming!
summary: Wave of Support creates a pathway to individual and collective mental wellbeing through a campus wide commitment to self-care and empathy. We promote self-advocacy, positive skill building, and a culture of deep attention to mental health at Tulane.

name: Fine Arts & Culture Society of TUSOM
summary: Our mission is to promote the appreciation of the incredible displays of human expression across this historic town and offer medical students an organized opportunity to explore all different kinds of art (painting, sculpture, film, song, dance, et al).

name: New Orleans Shakespeare Festival at Tulane
description:
The mission of the New Orleans Shakespeare Festival at Tulane is to produce professional, classical theatre with a primary focus upon the works of William Shakespeare. Our commitment is to utilize local, national, and international talent to create dynamic, visceral, text-based performances celebrating Shakespeare’s brilliant insight into the human condition. With this programming we will provide to the people of the Gulf South both entertainment and educational resources of the highest quality, honoring Shakespeare’s legacy.
Founded in 1993 by several Tulane Faculty members, the Festival has become one of the most significant theatre companies in the Gulf South. Each season typically consists of two mainstage productions, various lagniappe events, and our acclaimed Peformance for the Schools educational program. Programming is also taken to schools in the greater New Orleans area to awaken new generations to the power of language and literacy through live, professional performances of Shakespeare’s plays.
summary: New Orleans' classical theatre company in residence at Tulane University.

name: Tulane University Women's Association
description: TUWA membership is open to faculty, staff, alumni, and Tulane affiliates.
summary: The mission of the TUWA is to foster a strong and supportive community network honoring women at Tulane, and to help its members connect to the greater university community through shared priorities, social interests and service.

name: Tulane Law International Programs

name: Cowen Institute
description: The mission of the Cowen Institute at Tulane University is to equip leaders and practitioners with skills and resources to increase educational and postsecondary success among New Orleans student. Founded in 2007, the Cowen Institute was established to monitor, chronicle, and analyze the significant changes in New Orleans' public education system following Hurricane Katrina. The institute is named after Dr. Scott Cowen, President Emeritus of Tulane University, who played a key role in New Orleans’ recovery and the transformation of its education system. Today, our mission is fulfilled through two strategic pillars: contextualizing data to provide thought leadership and building practitioners' capacity to drive educational success.
summary: The mission of the Cowen Institute at Tulane University is to advance public education and college and career success for the New Orleans community.

name: International City/County Management Association
description:
Tulane ICMA is a concierge for career-building, helping both graduate and undergraduate students build careers in the public, civic, and private sectors.  
 
Our chapter -- the first in Louisiana – is a career pipeline that bridges our university community with the world by connecting students with professionals, career programming, and opportunities and by driving positive change through collaborative projects with employers.  Among the benefits that we offer are experiential learning, networking opportunities, mentorship, access to internships and scholarships, best practices and advancement education.  
 
Originally launched within Tulane SoPA's John Lewis Public Administration Program, our Tulane ICMA chapter is part of a broader network of more than 11,000 local government professionals worldwide who are dedicated to developing thriving communities via innovative solutions, idea sharing, and recruitment of talent through professional networks.  At Tulane, our alumni have emerged as leaders in fields, such as economic development, law, communications, healthcare, academia and educational leadership, and nonprofit management and fundraising - just to name a few!  Our current Tulane membership are interested in a wide range of career fields, including but not limited to federal, state, and local government; law; civic sector management; economic, workforce, and community development; environmental sustainability; technology policy; social entrepreneurship; public safety; international relations; and national security.  We are inclusive of all and embrace the values of diversity, equity, and inclusion. 
 
All are welcome and invited to join us.  We look forward to helping you build your dream career!
 
Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Tulane ICMA (International City / County Management Association) is part of a network of 11,000+ professionals worldwide dedicated to helping you build your dream career!

name: Middle East North African Studies
description: Drawing upon faculty strengths in Tulane’s School of Liberal Arts, faculty in Middle East and North African studies allow students to learn about a complex region through an interdisciplinary approach. Based in a number of departments and programs in the humanities and social sciences, faculty offer a wide array of courses to provide a broad background in the region’s past and present. By introducing students to the rich history, layered politics, diverse cultures, linguistic, and religious traditions of the Middle East and North Africa, Tulane’s faculty seek to transcend simplistic generalizations and stereotypes about the region and its people while promoting global literacy in an increasingly interconnected world.
summary: Program for the study of the Middle East and North African region.

name: Arabic Studies
description: Standard Arabic has an estimated 206 million speakers, while colloquial Arabic is spoken by a total of over 400 million, which makes it the fifth most widely spoken language in the world. Arabic classes at Tulane highlight the richness of Arabic culture and literature, as well as its practical applications in the global political economy.
summary: Program to support the learning of Arabic language and culture.

name: Medieval & Early Modern Studies Program
description: MEMS is a multidisciplinary program with a focus on the history and cultures of the medieval and early modern world. The Program consists of faculty and students from diverse departments within the School of Liberal Arts (Art History, Asian Studies, Classical Studies, English, French and Italian, Germanic and Slavic Studies, History, Jewish Studies, Music, Philosophy, Political Science, Spanish and Portuguese) and offers undergraduate major and minor concentrations, as well as graduate degree programs in affiliated professors' home departments. We regularly sponsor presentations by scholars from universities around the country, conferences, and colloquia.
summary: MEMS is a multidisciplinary program with a focus on the history and cultures of the medieval and early modern world.

name: Green Wave Films
description:
Green Wave Films is the premiere filmmaking club of Tulane, where Digital Media Practices students as well as students of other majors can learn about the filmmaking process, pitch their ideas, and work on set. GWF also provides networking opportunities, both with students and with professionals. With community-building events, workshops, and talented guest speakers, the club is a great opportunity to learn about and exercise a breadth of skills in entertainment.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Green Wave Films serves as a film production, distribution, and networking club, connecting all Tulane students with opportunities and learning experiences in the world of visual media.

name: Tulane Indigenous Students, Healers, Leaders and Allies
description:
ISHLA is an open group to all Tulane students and community members interested in Native American, American Indian, Alaska Native, and / or Indigenous cultures, issues, and relations. The group promotes awareness of Indigenous experiences and culture through education, community building, and creative awareness, promoting the needs of Indigenous students, Leaders, Helpers and Allies at Tulane University. The group aims to supports relations of Indigenous students with one another and the greater global Indigenous community.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: ISHLA is an open group to all Tulane community members interested in Native American, American Indian, Alaska Native, and / or Indigenous cultures, issues, and relations; aiming to celebrate and build community through connection with one another.

name: Tulane Global - Office of International Affairs
description: At Tulane, we are bold and audacious, and our impact knows no geographical bounds. As part of the vibrant and culturally rich New Orleans community, we cultivate diverse experiences and perspectives, locally and globally. We know today’s pressing global challenges demand comprehensive global solutions. As a global university and leader in research, Tulane’s international footprint boasts more than 70 programs across more than 70 countries. Tulane Global provides direction for the University’s international endeavors, collaborating across campus to support ground-breaking research, foster an inclusive international community and facilitate impactful global learning and engagement.
summary: Tulane Global provides direction for the University’s international endeavors, collaborating across campus to support ground-breaking research, foster an inclusive international community and facilitate impactful global learning and engagement.

name: TIERA Program
description:
To stay up-to-date on all TIERA programs and information sign up here! Learn more about the program on our website!

The TIERA Program is a collaboration between Tulane University and the Foundation for the Conservation of the Tropical Andes (FCAT), an Ecuadorian NGO. 

The program combines on-campus programing and community building with immersive fieldwork in an award-winning, community engaged conservation reserve in Ecuador. Students in TIERA gain hands-on experience and develop environmental leadership skills at a safe, modern “learning laboratory” in Ecuador. 

The TIERA program is open to undergraduate students of all majors and all years and also includes faculty and graduate students. Undergraduates interested in the program can apply to participate on a 2 week TIERA field course here.

Check out this short video about our partner, FCAT!

https://youtu.be/OWcWDmfwYlw
summary: The Tulane Interdisciplinary Environmental Research and Action (TIERA) Program is a socio-ecological leadership program that empowers students and faculty who care about ethical environmentalism to combine their scholarship with direct, real world impact

name: Environmental Studies Program
description: Environmental Studies focuses on understanding the relationships between humans and the environment. It develops a sophisticated understanding of the interplay between humans and the environment and rigorous knowledge of key environmental and humanistic concerns. The program prepares students to engage productively with the world around them: knowledgeable about people and the environment and armed with skills in critical thinking and analysis that prepare them well for careers in environmental education, advocacy, policy, research, and a wide range of other fields.
summary: Environmental Studies focuses on understanding the relationships between humans and the environment.

name: BridgeUSA
description:
BridgeUSA is a youth-led 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization that creates spaces on high school and college campuses for open discussion between students about political issues. Political division is one of our biggest challenges in the United States. Today’s youth are being taught that tribalism is better than unity and that having conversations across our differences is impossible. Spoiler Alert: This isn’t true!

We began in 2016 at the universities of Notre Dame, CU-Boulder, and UC Berkeley in response to growing polarization on campus. Our work emphasizes the importance of empathy and understanding, ideological diversity and solution-oriented politics. By engaging America’s youth in constructive discussions, we are equipping the next generation of leaders with the skills necessary for navigating conflict, finding solutions across differences and building bridges in their communities.

We are currently located on over 50 college campuses and 24 high school campuses in the U.S.

 

https://groupme.com/join_group/108936399/G48a6TFR

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: BridgeUSA is a youth-led 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization that creates spaces on high school and college campuses for open discussion between students about political issues.

name: Tulane For Every Body
description:
Our vision is to hold space for conversation around health, body image, and size in a safe and welcoming space, while introducing the tools necessary to implement body respect and self-compassion practices in the everyday lives of students. Tulane for Every Body runs regular workshop sessions, events, and projects that dive deep into personal experiences surrounding these issues. Our goal is to encourage others to embrace all aspects of themselves in a society that tells them they are never enough.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Tulane for Every Body is a student-led and staff-supervised initiative at Tulane University aimed at bringing awareness and advocating for the inclusion, acceptance, and support of all people regardless of body size and shape.

name: Tulane Consulting Group
description:
The Tulane Consulting Group (TCG), a selective student-run undergraduate organization, provides a space for students to learn the essential skills needed to thrive in a consulting role: data analytics, presentation, communication, and case study skills. We also teach our members how to improve their professional profiles by conducting resume and cover letter workshops. Finally, the TCG hosts guest speaker events and case competitions as an opportunity for students to show off their consulting skills and build connections with professionals in the industry.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The TCG is established to provide a space for students to build connections with professionals in the management consulting industry, learn the essential skills needed to obtain and thrive in an advisory role, and develop their professional profiles.

The Culinary Medicine Initiative aims to provide a community interested in exploring the links between food and health. Monthly meetings range from volunteering at the Goldring Center for Medicine to gathering and cooking a shared meal together. Please email Mitchell Cohen (mcohen25@tulane.edu) with any questions!

A book will be selected by the members for the month which will be discussed at the end of the month. Benvenuti! IACTU exists to foster a growing relationship between Tulane students and the Italian-American community in New Orleans as well as to build connections between Tulane students of Italian-American heritage and those who are interested in Italian-American culture. Many of us engage with the Italian language through the participation in courses taught by the Department of French & Italian. We do a number of activities, ranging from bocce to culinary appreciation.

name: American Chemical Society Tulane Chapter
description:
Welcome to the Tulane chapter of the American Chemical Society. The ACS was Founded in 1876 and chartered by the U.S. Congress. ACS is one of the world’s largest scientific organizations with more than 173,000 individuals in the global community across 140 countries. The ACS' mission is to advance the broader chemistry enterprise and its practitioners to benefit Earth and its people. 

Tulane's chapter will allow students with a passion for chemistry to meet professionals in the field and interact with other like-minded students during events, speaker presentations, and meetings. 

If you have any questions or concerns please contact Maiyan Suskin or Britta Pellegrin. Emails can be found in the members' section of the club page. To stay up to date with any events or announcements join our Group Me

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Tulane chapter of the American Chemical Society, an international organization dealing with publications, conventions, and other events in chemistry.

name: Association of Latino Professionals for America Student Chapter at Tulane University
shortName: ALPFA Student Chapter at Tulane University
description:
The mission of ALPFA at Tulane University is to empower and develop Latino leaders within the Tulane community. Our goal is to create a supportive and inclusive environment that enables our members to achieve their full potential and make a positive impact on campus and beyond. We achieve this by: 

  1. Providing opportunities for our members to develop professionally through networking events, workshops, and mentorship programs. 
  2. Building relationships with corporate partners and other organizations to create employment opportunities and promote diversity and inclusion in the workplace. 
  3. Supporting the growth and success of Latino students through leadership development, skills training, and advocacy. 
  4. Promoting cultural awareness and appreciation for the Latino community through various events and activities. 

Overall, our mission is to create a strong community of Latino leaders within Tulane University and beyond, who are equipped with the skills and resources they need to succeed in their chosen fields and make a positive impact on society. 

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: ALPFA is an organization that seeks to provide professional development workshops and career resources to Latin American members of each university. There goal is to increase networking and provide opportunities for Latino members in the United States.

summary: Tulane Law's Career Development staff members provide personalized counseling, conduct workshops on job search strategies, resumes and cover letter review, mock interviews, and networking techniques.

name: Student Activity Fee Allocation Council
description: The Student Activity Fee Allocation Council (SAFAC) allocates student activity fees to enable and empower a student created and led, thriving community of Recognized Student Organizations (RSOs) and student experience for all students. In alignment with our EDI statement, the SAFAC applies equitable funding decisions to represent the interest of the undergraduate students and enhances campus life by supporting the mission and values of Tulane. The SAFAC distributes funds annually to Council Allocation Committees (Club Sports, CACTUS, Multicultural Council/Religious, Gender and Sexuality Advisory Council, Student Organization Center, and Graduate and Professional Student Association), and as needed through the Reserve Funding Projects.
summary: The Student Activity Fee Allocation Council allocates student activity fees to enable and empower a student created and led, thriving community of Recognized Student Organizations (RSOs) and student experience for all students.

name: The Women's Network
description:
The Women’s Network (TWN) was created for ambitious, high-achieving women looking to grow professionally while being uplifted by their community. TWN offers opportunities to gain exposure to the professional world, network with high-profile speakers, and meet peers across their campus and the nation. TWN meets monthly and holds speaker events, various networking events, career development workshops (e.g., resume and LinkedIn workshops), alumnae receptions, and interactive activities to develop relationships among members. Our guest speakers and workshops will help prepare you for your next steps and may open your eyes to career paths you haven't even considered. With over 100 chapters and national oversight, the organization is continuously expanding, as are its opportunities for members - all free of charge.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The largest collegiate women's networking organization in the nation – cultivating and celebrating women's ambition through connecting our members to industry leaders, professional development resources, and career opportunities.

name: Wall Street Krewe
description:
Wall Street Krewe brings together people with a common interest in asset management, equity research, and other finance professions associated with Wall Street. Our club activities include discussions of asset management, discussion of equity research and stock investing, and career advancement content. We also host speakers and provide networking opportunities for members and the broader community.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Wall Street Krewe brings together people interested in investment banking, asset and wealth management, equity research, and other finance professions. We strive to provide meaningful programming to help students become informed about career paths.

name: Girl Gains Tulane University
description:
Girl Gains is a rapidly-expanding nationwide network of groups with the goal of making the often male-dominated gym more accessible to other genders. It emphasizes empowerment through knowledge and a positive community. Members of GG will attend guest speaker meetings, workshops, group workouts, and other group activities. They will meet members across the country and join a network of likeminded others. This organization stands out in that it focuses on weightlifting and strength and comes along with a plethora of already-established resources. 

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Girl Gains aims to promote accessible weightlifting and empower women. It was created to provide lifters of all backgrounds, gender identities, and fitness levels with community, resources, and confidence.

description:
This dance organization primarily focuses on HBCU and majorette styles of dance which comes from the Southern Black perspective. We will focus on technique, creating stands, routines, field shows, and more! We will be performing at Tulane/Loyola events such as, but not limited to, football games, basketball games, and parades.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

name: Phi Delta Theta
description:
Phi Delta Theta was built on three pillars that haven’t budged an inch since the Fraternity was founded by “The Immortal Six” way back in 1848 at Miami University. The pillars are: The cultivation of friendship among its members, the acquirement individually of a high degree of mental culture, and the attainment personally of a high standard of morality. Shortened, we refer to the principles as friendship, sound learning and rectitude.

Helping every individual to meet his true potential is the bedrock of the Phi Delta Theta Fraternity. By celebrating each person’s true self, and by learning from each other’s strengths while helping to improve each other’s weaknesses, every member of Phi Delta Theta develops into a greater version of himself than he could ever on his own. Rather than try to find young men to “mold” into some ideal, we celebrate the uniqueness of each individual and, through encouragement, values, example and brotherhood, empower every brother to exceed his personal expectations.
summary: Phi Delta Theta is a fraternity that focuses on our three main pillars of  friendship, sound learning and rectitude.

description: Welcome to the Department of Economics at Tulane University. The main areas in which faculty members conduct research and teach courses are development economics (focusing on inequality and poverty), the economics of education, health economics, and public economics, as well as in economic history, international economics, and core areas of econometrics and economic theory. The Department has 19 regular faculty members, 4 postdoctoral teaching fellows, 2 visiting or adjunct professors, over 300 undergraduate majors, almost 40 graduate students, and 4 affiliated research centers.

name: Tulane School of Medicine - Department of Pediatrics - Section of Adolescent Medicine
description:
The Section of Adolescent Medicine provides a wide variety of health care settings to introduce residents and students to the care of adolescents, service to the Department of Pediatrics providing continuity of care clinics and referrals to sub-specialists, leadership in the research and care of adolescents with HIV, the provision of accredited college health programs, leadership in healthcare for homeless youth, and leadership in street and community outreach.  Tulane's Section of Adolescent Medicine provided the only Title X reproductive health clinic on the East Bank of New Orleans.

Vision

We are a model of excellence creating a world where all young people are celebrated, thriving, and achieving their full potential.

Mission

We foster innovative community-engaged research, education, advocacy and comprehensive healthcare for young people.
summary: In the Section of Adolescent Medicine at the Tulane School of Medicine, we foster innovative community-engaged research, education, advocacy, and comprehensive healthcare for young people.

name: Connolly Alexander Institute for Data Science
description:
At the Connolly Alexander Institute for Data Science at Tulane University, we recognize the paramount importance of data literacy in today's world. In an era driven by data, possessing the skills to effectively navigate and comprehend information is vital.

Data literacy entails not only the ability to analyze numerical data and visualizations but also the capacity to critically evaluate, interpret, and communicate data insights. With technology advancing at an unprecedented pace and the exponential growth of data, developing a solid foundation in data literacy has become essential.

Our institute is dedicated to cultivating a comprehensive understanding of data science concepts and methodologies among the Tulane community. By fostering data literacy, we aim to empower individuals to tackle real-world challenges and drive innovation in the data-driven landscape.
summary: By fostering data literacy, the Connolly Alexander Institute for Data Science aims to empower individuals to tackle real-world challenges and drive innovation in the data-driven landscape.

The Office of Family Programs supports this partnership with a variety of communication resources, events on campus, information, and assistance. We are committed to providing you with valuable, timely information both online and in person. Find more details at Office of Family Programs | Tulane Families.

To help your student learn to manage their academic and personal success, we recommend that you coach and support your student and encourage him or her to take steps to find information, utilize helpful resources and solve problems. summary: The Office of Tulane Family Programs provides support, information, newsletters, webinars, and events for the families of undergraduate students at Tulane.

name: Department of Anthropology
description: The mission of the Department of Anthropology is to provide undergraduate and graduate instruction in anthropology, and to produce innovative research and scholarship. Key to our research and teaching mission is maintaining integration and balance among anthropology's four fields: biological anthropology, sociocultural anthropology, and linguistic anthropology, and anthropological archaeology. Through research and publication, and the teaching and mentoring of undergraduate and graduate students, we strive to advance and disseminate knowledge of human and nonhuman primate origins and behavior, major developments in human prehistory, mechanisms of cultural change, human variation and adaptation, and an understanding and appreciation of modern cultural, biological, and linguistic diversity.
summary: The mission of the Department of Anthropology is to provide undergraduate and graduate instruction in anthropology, and to produce innovative research and scholarship.

name: Carolyn Barber-Pierre Center for Intercultural Life - Tulane University
description:
The Carolyn Barber-Pierre Center for Intercultural Life is a dynamic center for cultural, social, and intellectual engagement at Tulane University. We foster connection, reflection, and celebration across diverse cultures, identities, and life experiences. While our focus is on uplifting student communities that have historically faced barriers in higher education, our programs and resources are open to all.

Through mentorship, leadership development, cultural events, and community-building initiatives, we help students cultivate a strong sense of belonging on campus. In collaboration with students, faculty, and staff, the Center plays a vital role in shaping a university culture where every individual is respected, supported, and empowered to thrive.
summary: The Carolyn Barber-Pierre Center for Intercultural Life fosters a welcoming environment for all students, with programs, services, and support that center on the experiences of historically underserved communities.

name: Tulane Institute for Physician Scientists
summary: The Tulane Institute for Physician Scientists (TIPS) is designed to foster and support the growing community of physician scientists at Tulane.

name: Native American Studies
summary: The Native American Studies minor offers students the opportunity to increase their knowledge of indigenous peoples of the Americas, their (pre)history, their lifeways, their languages, and the issues they face today. Active engagement in co-construction

name: Tulane Office of Global Health
description:
The mission of the Tulane Office of Global Health (OGH) is to foster interdisciplinary, collaborative research opportunities for faculty across Tulane University and to promote global health research training opportunities for Tulane students. Offices are located in the School of Public Health and Tropical Medicine and in an overseas office in Lima, Peru (HOLA, or the Health Office for Latin America).

 

In this era of interdisciplinary global health, research teams pooling expertise in diverse areas, such as human behavior, design of the built environment, ecology and climate, biomedical engineering, social and cultural mores, clinical tropical medicine, and health access and societal rights, global health is not just of interest to faculty in medicine and public health. It is an increasingly important focus area for universities, with potential relevance and impact for faculty and students from schools within Tulane University.

 

The Tulane Office of Global Health includes representation from across the university, including many of the schools (see OGH Steering Committee, below). Additionally, the Office of Global Health has collaborative relationships with international organizations from a variety of countries, including Argentina, Cuba, the Dominican Republic, Peru, Suriname, and Sierra Leone.

 

For faculty, OGH provides support through:

  • Hosting global health networking events to bring faculty interested in global health together and formulate working groups able to respond to global health initiatives. Expertise in global health at Tulane spans multiple schools, including Science and Engineering, Social Work, Liberal Arts, Public Health, and Medicine.
  • Providing guidance for faculty on developing research and training program proposals with global health partners, special considerations for international research, and how to create a viable implementation plan for research based overseas.
  • Providing supplemental administrative support for multidisciplinary global health training grants for NIH, the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, and other major donor agencies.
  • Providing seed money for short-term travel or meetings to facilitate new global health research collaborations or grant applications.
  • Sponsoring university-wide presentations on global health topics for faculty and students.

For students, OGH provides support through:

  • Administrating and publicizing global health research training opportunities provided by training grants led by Tulane faculty. (NOTE: OGH does not maintain a database of global health research opportunities for students that are not part of Tulane-led training grants, and it does not manage study abroad programs.)
  • Supporting global health interest groups across the university.
summary: The mission of the Tulane Office of Global Health (OGH) is to foster interdisciplinary, collaborative research opportunities for faculty across Tulane University and to promote global health research training opportunities for Tulane students.

name: Tulane Living Well Clinic
description:
Tulane Living Well Clinics offers a ranges of additional services including biometrics, flu and COVID-19 vaccines, and smoking cessation. The wellness clinic is staffed with a nurse practitioner. Employees requiring primary care services from a staff physician will be responsible for a co-pay. Some appointments can be conducted via telehealth, including minor urgent care and health coaching appointments.

The Tulane Living Well Clinic offers a metaboli care program that includes a multifaceted approach to weight management. The plan involves a 360 degree systematic view, including targeted intervention that have been proven to help people improve their health outcomes for issues like high blood pressure, high cholesterol, diabetes and other chronic conditions.  The offerings include, but are not limited to health coaching, dietetic counseling cognitive behavioral therapy, as well as resources for exercise. The plan is unique and patient-centered and tailored in its approach.

Wellness Support, Health Coaching & Primary Care - With a Personal Touch

  • The Tulane Living Well Clinics are centrally located and accessible by all faculty and staff enrolled in a Tulane University Health Plan. With locations both Uptown and Downtown, the Living Well Clinics are available to assist with same-day appointments for urgent care needs, as well as wellness visits including flu shots, biometrics, and long-term health coaching.

    The clinic offers dietetic counseling for patients with high blood pressure, diabetes, cholesterol, and other chronic conditions; it also offers health coaching in the following areas:

    Nutrition basics
    Navigating fast food and eating out
    Reducing sodium in your diet
    Understanding and learning how to read nutrition labels
    Meal planning, calories and portions
    Exercise planning
summary: Tulane Living Well Clinics are located on both the Uptown and Downtown campuses of Tulane University for all faculty and staff employees offering no cost, same day appointments for urgent care needs,  scheduled health coaching and wellness visits.

summary: The Tulane University Center for Continuing Education (CCE)  is accredited by the ACCME® to provide AMA PRA Category 1 Credits™ and by IACET to offer general CEUs. CCE collaborates with departments or organizations to provide conference management.

name: Epsilon Eta Sigma
description:
Founded in 2023, Epsilon Eta is a professional fraternity at Tulane University for those seeking post-grad positions in the environmental field. We seek to develop a more robust network of students, researchers, employers, and agencies to more effectively deal with the realities of our world’s changing climate and loss of biodiversity. By fostering a conscious awareness of the intrinsic relationship between people and their environment through academics, the community, and service, we seek to bridge the gap from the undergraduate atmosphere to professional environmental fields for students at Tulane. As an organization, we do not exclude based on major or school, but require an interest in the environmental field. 

 

Epsilon Eta Sigma upholds three pillars consistent with the national pillars. These are professional development, volunteerism, and social. 

 

Professional: We strive to prepare our members for careers in the environmental field by providing a hub for networking, support, and professional development. By facilitating resume workshops, offering professional head shots, and hosting speakers who work in a variety of fields, Epsilon Eta aims to be the foundation for our members’ futures working in environmental careers.

 

Volunteerism: Our service focuses on restoration, community action, activism, and environmental justice in and around the local New Orleans area.

 

Social: Fostering a strong community is what gives Epsilon Eta momentum. Our bonds allow us to better understand each other and our many viewpoints in order to better consider those from across the environmental movement and advance the larger conversation on sustainability.

 

We use a recruitment style registration and plan to begin in spring of 2024. 

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Epsilon Eta is the national co-ed professional fraternity for students seeking post-grad positions in the environmental field. Epsilon Eta Sigma serves that purpose on Tulane's campus through our three pillars: Professional, volunteerism, and social.

name: Cinemates of Tulane University
description: Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: “Cinemates of Tulane University” is established for the purpose of bringing together people who share a common appreciation of film. “Cinemates of Tulane University” calls on all cinephiles to collectively explore new forms of film.

name: Furries at Tulane
description:
The club for Furries at Tulane is a group for people to meet, discuss, and make friends with interests pertaining to anthropomorphic animals. Furry hobbies include but are not limited to cosplaying, drawing, crafting, storytelling, song-writing, and attending conventions. The furry fandom originated in the 1980s as a subsection of science fiction and comic conventions, but quickly grew into its own community. Because of the diversity within the fandom, as well as fictional media portrayals based on stereotypes, there is a certain amount of stigma surrounding furries. We seek to positively portray the community as the inclusive, uplifting, creative, and supportive space it is.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Furries at Tulane is a club for like-minded individuals to connect and build friendships centered around anthropomorphic animals. Activities include cosplaying, drawing, crafting, storytelling, songwriting, and attending conventions.

name: Big Easy EC
description:
Big Easy EC is a group of dedicated volunteers committed to reproductive justice and healthcare. Big Easy EC provides and delivers free and confidential emergency contraception, pregnancy tests, and barrier methods to Tulane University and Loyola University students. Big Easy EC provides three (3) materials to requesters: emergency contraception, pregnancy tests, and condoms. The purpose of Big Easy EC is to advocate for reproductive justice and provide students with the ease and comfort of a personalized delivery of any requested kit. Big Easy EC seeks to achieve its goal of promoting reproductive justice through outreach events. On account of partnership with the Newcomb Institute and under the direction of an advisor, Big Easy EC is hoping to expand its mission through school-sanctioned recruitment of volunteers and advertisement.

If you are interested in becoming a volunteer, we recruit during the start of each fall semester. Follow our Instagram, @bigeasyec, for application and recruitment timeline updates, as well as updates on our services!

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary:
Big Easy EC is a group of dedicated volunteers committed to reproductive justice and
healthcare. Big Easy EC provides and delivers free and confidential emergency contraception,
pregnancy tests, and barrier methods to Tulane and Loyola students.

name: Tulane Center for Community-Engaged Artificial Intelligence
description:
Artificial intelligence holds the potential to benefit society in numerous areas, including health, the economy, education, and public safety. However, ensuring that the benefits of AI are shared equitably among society and reducing any potential harmful effects is of critical importance.  Across the country, there is a growing tech backlash concerned that AI may exacerbate existing disparities, widen the digital divide, or otherwise result in a less just society. In response, the scientific community has increasingly prioritized research into socio-technical systems that promote fair and trustworthy AI systems.

These trends indicate that AI will succeed only with the trust and support of the communities it affects, especially those from historically underserved groups. The motivating principle of the Center is that this trust must be earned by designing AI systems (1) that are demonstrably fair, transparent, and accountable, (2) that establish meaningful relationships to engage diverse communities in all stages of the AI process, from design through deployment, and (3) that augment, rather than replace, human interaction, i.e., are human centered. Achieving this vision requires a transformative approach to AI design. The Center will establish a truly multi-disciplinary team of technologists, designers, social scientists, and community partners for examining and affording new evidence-based socio-technical frameworks to create and deploy AI systems that are inclusive, effective, fair, transparent, and accountable.
summary: Research Center of Excellence investigating new approaches to artificial intelligence that are inclusive, effective, fair, transparent, and accountable.

name: Enterprise Risk Services
description:
Tulane began implementation of a new risk management strategy, known as enterprise risk management (ERM). This approach engages the university community in planning for and responding to risks large and small that can disrupt its critical operations, vital research or educational mission.

Office of Enterprise Risk Services | Office of Enterprise Risk Services (tulane.edu)
summary: The Office of Enterprise Risk Services provides risk, insurance, and environmental expertise as well as emergency management and business continuity services through customized programs and innovative solutions.

name: Group on Women in Medicine and Science
description: Tulane’s GWIMS chapter, established in August 2018 with support from the Tulane SOM Dean's Office, advocates for these shared goals by providing a space for the promotion of dialogue surrounding what it means to be a woman in medicine and science, supporting the health and wellness of our medical community, and creating recognition, mentorship, and networking opportunities. We are proud to serve as a resource and provide guidance for search committees, deans, and department chairs to promote faculty recruitment and career advancement practices that are inclusive of women.
summary: An accredited AAMC Affinity Group with the mission to advance the full and successful participation of women in all roles within academic medicine by addressing gender equity, recruitment and retention, awards and recognition, and career advancement.

name: University Compliance & Ethics
description:
ulane’s Compliance & Ethics Program is integrated into the university-wide Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) program.

The ERM program is a collaborative risk-management effort supported by the work of the Office of Enterprise Risk Services, Internal Audit, the Office of the General Counsel, and the Compliance & Ethics Work Group, in coordination with the University’s Governance, Risk, and Compliance Executive Council (GRCEC), a standing committee of University executives. The GRCEC's responsibilities include, among others, promoting the appropriate “Tone at the Top” and “Mood at the Middle” that fully supports a culture of enterprise risk management, compliance, ethical behavior, and non-retaliation at Tulane, including effective communication of such throughout the organization. The GRCEC also provides oversight and evaluation of the structure, operations, and effectiveness of the Compliance & Ethics Program.

The Compliance & Ethics Work Group supports and coordinates ethics and compliance functions across the University.
summary: Tulane's Compliance & Ethics Program supports and coordinates ethics and compliance functions across the University.

name: Department of Earth & Environmental Sciences
summary: The Department of Earth and Environmental Sciences at Tulane has existed under its current name since about 2003.  Prior to that, the department was known as the Department of Geology.  Geology was first studied at Tulane within the chemistry department.

name: Tulane River and Coastal Center
summary: Part of the ByWater Institute, the Tulane River and Coastal Center features laboratories, offices, and a public meeting space with views of the Mississippi. We host events and organizations relevant to our mission. You can reach us at trcc@tulane.edu.

name: Badminton Club
description: • The name of this organization shall be Badminton Club at Tulane University. Badminton club may also be referenced as BCTU in publicity materials and correspondence.
summary: Badminton club is established for the purpose of creating a fun and inclusive environment for all badminton lovers. We meet twice a week on Mondays and Wednesdays from 6-8PM

name: Ryan's Giving Tree Club
description:
Every Saturday Morning, RGT goes to homeless and low income communities with breakfast foods, coffee, and other essential goods. We distribute the items to homeless people in the New Orleans area and have conversations with them. We build connections with these individuals by returning to the same neighborhoods weekly. We are trying to create a better life and opportunity for the low-income community of New Orleans, which is why we want to start an on-campus club. Starting a club on campus will allow us to continue our work toward our mission after we all graduate college. We want to expand our volunteer base and for Tulane students to continue being part of RGT's community. It will also help provide the financial resources to continue serving the homeless on Saturday mornings and have events and fundraisers to maximize the good we do for the New Orleans community. By joining the RGT team, you will surround yourself with a great group of volunteers and see firsthand the impactful work you are doing.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Ryan’s Giving Tree (RGT) is a community organizer, focused on engaging communities, collecting data, and fostering unity in the simple goal of achieving health, wealth, and housing for the marginalized groups of New Orleans.

name: Future Minds AI
description:
The Future Minds AI Club is a community dedicated to the exploration, understanding, and advancement of artificial intelligence. Founded by enthusiasts passionate about the transformative power of AI, the club believes in both the immediate and future benefits this technology promises. 
 
Key Club Activities
 
Expert-Led Sessions: The club offers workshops and seminars led by AI professionals. These sessions dive into foundational AI principles, the latest industry trends, and the forefront of AI research. 
 
Hands-On Projects: Members have the opportunity to engage in practical AI projects. Working collaboratively in teams, they tackle real-world challenges spanning various sectors. This not only enhances understanding but also bridges the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical application. We will also be particularly looking into the business side of AI. 
 
Networking: The Greater Minds AI Club is a nexus for AI enthusiasts. Here, members can connect, exchange ideas, and even collaborate on research papers and other projects. Exclusive Resources: Club members have access to a curated collection of resources. This includes articles, tutorials, and specialized software tools, all aimed at bolstering their journey in the AI landscape. 
 

Partnership Announcement: We are excited to announce a budding partnership with OIP Robotics, a renowned AI firm. Once the club is fully operational, OIP Robotics has committed to offering their expertise and support, ensuring our club members have a comprehensive and professional learning experience. For more details, visit https://oiprobotics.com/. 

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The goal of Future Minds AI is to keep students up to date on the latest AI trends via learning and working with AI. This goal will be achieved via listening to speakers on the topic of AI, hand-on projects, net working and many other avenues.

name: Education Research Alliance
description: The Education Research Alliance for New Orleans (ERA-New Orleans) is a research center that collaborates with local education stakeholders to produce objective, rigorous, and useful research to inform the community’s understanding of how to improve students’ experiences in schools and beyond. ERA-New Orleans is housed in the School of Liberal Arts at Tulane University and supported by funding from Tulane's Murphy Institute.
summary: The Education Research Alliance for New Orleans (ERA-New Orleans) is a research center that collaborates with local education stakeholders to produce objective, rigorous, and useful research.

name: International Student Advisory Board
description:
The goal of the ISAB is to represent the needs and concerns of international students at Tulane and to work towards improving the Tulane experience for all international students.

The ISAB advises the Office of International Students and Scholars (OISS) on international student needs and concerns, participates in the development of new initiatives, interacts with the wider University community to raise the visibility of international students, and develops international student leadership.

Board members will provide feedback for programming, including but not limited to social programming, orientation programs, information sessions, immigration support, and website improvements. In addition, board members will have the opportunity to comment on the overall experience of being a student at Tulane. International students need to know that their voices and ideas are understood, and OISS is responsible for actively integrating ISAB's feedback, concerns, and ideas into general programming and goals.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The ISAB raises international student needs and concerns, participates in developing new initiatives, interacts with the wider University community to raise the visibility of international students, and develops international student leadership.

TME is a vibrant and inclusive community that thrives on the virtual landscapes of Minecraft, where students come together to build, explore, and connect in a digital world of limitless possibilities.

description:
The Tulane Poker Club is a student-run organization at Tulane University that aims to promote the game of poker among the student body. The club hosts regular meetings and events, such as poker tournaments and workshops, to allow students to learn and improve their poker skills. Additionally, the club participates in regional and national collegiate poker tournaments to showcase the talent of its members. The club is open to all Tulane students, regardless of experience level, and strives to create a fun and inclusive environment for all members.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

name: Tulane University School of Medicine Department of Orthopaedic Surgery
description:
At Tulane University School of Medicine, we have a dual role of education and service. Patient care is provided by our outstanding faculty and resident staff. Our faculty are experts in orthopaedic problems for patients of any age, whether it is a musculoskeletal injury or a painful condition, such as arthritis.

Patients are seen at Tulane Medical Center, Tulane Institute of Sports Medicine, Tulane-Lakeside Hospital, Tulane Sports Medicine Plus, University Medical Center (UMC) and the V.A. clinics.

Our educational mission is to teach our residents, fellows, and medical students about all aspects of the musculoskeletal system. We have a fully accredited 5-year Orthopaedic Residency. Our doctors teach Tulane University Medical Students and Sports Medicine Fellows. Through presentations at regional, national, and international orthopaedic meetings, our doctors share their knowledge with doctors all over the world.
summary: Our educational mission is to teach our residents, fellows, and medical students about all aspects of the musculoskeletal system. We have a fully accredited 5-year Orthopaedic Residency.

name: ReCoast
description: The ReCoast organization's vision is to create coastal community recycling programs to keep glass out of landfills and instead convert it into glass sand products that support coastal restoration, preservation, and resiliency. Through extensive regional economic, social, cultural, and environmental research, we are ensuring ecological safety and mitigation of land loss.
summary: Formed under the National Science Foundation Convergence Accelerator Program, ReCoast members are faculty, students, staff, alumni, and community collaborators working with Glass Half Full to recycle glass into sand for coastal restoration.

EESA accomplishes these goals by hosting various cultural celebrations, events, and activities. 

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

The Tulane University Cajun and Louisiana Creole Association was founded by two students who wanted to create a space to embrace the unique Cajun and Creole cultures of Louisiana. TUCALCA's mission is to help Tulane students engage with these cultures locally through community events focused on food, culture, language, and music. Some of these events include Cajun and Creole food tastings, language-centered gatherings, dance lessons, music performances, and cooking classes. All students are welcome to join TUCALCA, regardless of their own cultural identities.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

name: Healthy Celebrations Run Club
description:
Healthy Celebrations is a chain women's running club that is already present in over 40 universities. The club is a group of Tulane students, primarily undergraduate and graduate students who meet for biweekly runs around the beautiful garden district. We might sign up for local races and all participate together. It is a great opportunity to make new friendships and promote a healthy lifestyle. It is also a great opportunity to become more familiar with and learn about the city.

description:
1. Just Dance Club is intended to cultivate friendship and community through a laid-back, inclusive, positive dance atmosphere open to all! Our events can also serve as a wonderful study break and a mental health outlet. 

  2. Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Just Dance Club is open to all students, faculty, and staff of Tulane University who are searching for a community atmosphere to engage in stress-relieving, casual follow-along dance routines.

name: Sexual Violence Prevention and Response Collective
description:
SVPR, short for Sexual Violence Prevention & Response, is a small (and growing!) collective of dedicated activists seeking to address and respond to rape culture on Tulane’s campus. As the name suggests, SVPR pursues initiatives that are both prevention and response-facing in its efforts to decrease the presence of sexual violence and support survivors in accessing resources and care. We are an open committee, meaning that we have no application or requirements to join, and you can join us at any point in the semester and we will help you get familiarized with and involved in ongoing initiatives. We meet weekly from 7-8pm on Wednesdays in Newcomb Institute room 335 (3rd floor Commons). Our executive team also meets weekly on Mondays. 

 

To organize some of our main functions, we primarily create & implement initiatives, hold special events, and approve All In grants. First, our initiatives are created and implemented by our members, and overseen by our Directors. The Director of Prevention, Ian Michelson, oversees all prevention-focused initiatives, and the Director of Response, Anna Johnson, oversees all response-focused initiatives. One past initiative we have worked on was a content warning guide for journalists and activists, published in the Hullabaloo in our “Letter to the Editor | Reporting on Sexual Violence” Part 1 and Part 2. These articles reflected on how the university has reported on sexual violence in the past, and how it could improve. We created a content warning guide linked in those articles to assist journalists in more appropriately warning readers of descriptions of sexual violence. Another example of an initiative we did last year was a 3-day tabling campaign where we passed out collective-designed resources including “Self-Care: Tips & Tricks,” “Key Definitions Regarding Sexual Violence,” and “Navigating Support Following Sexual Violence at Tulane.” We shared these resources the week of the Climate Survey release to give students some information and resources before a heavy event filled with emotionally draining statistics and the potential to cause students to reflect on their own uncomfortable experiences. 

 

Second, SVPR holds special events. The largest and most profound of these is the Annual Sexual Violence Town Hall, in which we gather a panel of professionals and students who work with prevention and response work every day. The audience members anonymously ask questions, and the panel is preceded by opening remarks and a keynote speaker. This year, this event had over 100 attendees, our largest showing of the past 8 iterations of the town hall. Last year, we also held an event during Sex Week, in which two executive board members led the first run of the SAPHE (Un)Healthy Relationships Workshop. Another event we held last year called “Demystifying the Conduct Process” involved a presentation from a Tulane investigator, Jaquelyn Barber, who broke down the Title IX & Major Matters conduct processes with a Q&A at the end. 

 

Thirdly, a special privilege of SVPR is to review and vote to approve or deny All In grants. All In is Tulane’s programming to combat and respond to sexual violence at Tulane involving a detailed All In 2.0 plan. All In funds student programmatic opportunities and inclusive initiatives that align with this goal. As a collective, SVPR meets with grant applicants to review, propose changes to, and vote to approve funding. For example, SVPR was thrilled to fund Masked Violence, a collective art exhibition fighting sexual violence created and organized by Sophi Tomasulo. 

 

SVPR can pursue all these initiatives and goals due to our highly functional horizontal leadership structure. We have 5 executive team members, Director of Prevention: Ian Michelson, Director of Response: Anna Johnson, Chair of Social Media and Outreach: Aditi Morumganti, and our Secretary: Joanna Fashjian. We meet weekly as a team to plan out general body meetings and coordinate communication for ongoing initiatives and events. Our horizontal leadership allows us to delegate or take on extra tasks when necessary, always ensuring that we fulfill our weekly goals.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: SVPR is a collective of dedicated activists seeking to address and respond to rape culture at Tulane. We pursue initiatives that are both prevention and response facing to decrease the presence of sexual violence and support survivors.

name: Center for Inter-American Policy and Research
description:
The Center for Inter-American Policy and Research (CIPR) is devoted to inter-hemispheric exchanges that will advance the production and dissemination of knowledge about critical policy issues facing the Americas as well as the deepening of academic research in these areas. Its aim is to stimulate contact between scholars and decision-makers working on the region at different locations and in different languages, enriching their production by enabling the confluence of multiple perspectives.

CIPR focuses on issues of importance to Latin America and inter-American relations. Although this implies a dynamic agenda that will constantly evolve, its focus broadly encompasses aspects of political and human development as well as inter-American relations.

CIPR also seeks to raise awareness about the importance of Inter-American relations and contemporary policy debates. For this purpose, it engages business and civic leaders as well as members of the broader community in discussions related to its interest areas.
summary: CIPR is devoted to inter-hemispheric exchanges that will advance the production and dissemination of knowledge about critical policy issues facing the Americas as well as the deepening of academic research in these areas.

The Ph.D. degree requires a student to reach a critical understanding of the basic scientific and engineering principles underlying their field of interest. A 4+1 Master’s Program option is available to Tulane undergraduates.

For additional details, visit the graduate department/program website.
summary: The CBE's focus is to provide the highest quality programs to educate students at all levels in the principles and applications of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, within the environment of Tulane University.

name: Women of Public Health
description:
Welcome to Women of Public Health, a vibrant college organization offering women a dedicated space to share experiences and expertise across diverse public health industries. Our community thrives on collaboration and knowledge-sharing, providing members with invaluable insights through engaging events featuring accomplished guest speakers. These influential women leaders share their journeys, challenges, and successes, fostering an environment that promotes personal and professional growth.

Women of Public Health is more than just a platform; it's a supportive network committed to empowering women in the pursuit of excellence in public health. Our regular speaker series serves as a catalyst for inspiration and connection, allowing members to glean firsthand insights into the multifaceted aspects of the field.

In addition to our dynamic events, Women of Public Health is your go-to resource hub for information on opportunities within the realm of public health. Whether you're seeking internships, research positions, or exploring diverse career pathways, our organization strives to connect members with the resources they need to thrive.

Join us in shaping the future of public health through a community that celebrates the diverse contributions of women. Women of Public Health is your invitation to learn, connect, and empower one another, creating lasting impacts on the health and well-being of communities worldwide. Be part of the journey; join Women of Public Health today!

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

name: Stuart and Suzanne Grant Center for the American Jewish Experience
shortName: Grant Center for the American Jewish Experience
description:
A boundary-breaking hub that explores and shares the complexity of the American Jewish Experience with scholars, students, and the public.

Tulane’s Stuart and Suzanne Grant Center for the American Jewish Experience builds upon the strengths of Tulane University and the Department of Jewish Studies, operating as a world-class hub for exploring the varied and diverse nature of the American Jewish Experience.

The Grant Center contributes richness to the field of American Jewish studies by supporting experimentative, collaborative and interdisciplinary programs and research at the intersections of history, literature, cultural studies, sociology and other disciplines. The Center supports efforts that cross boundaries between academic spaces and the wider ecology of the Jewish world. Through co-curricular programming, research opportunities, and internships, Tulane students both contribute to and benefit from a holistic understanding of the historical and contemporary experience of American Jews. In addition, the Center’s ethos, events, scholarly projects and communications is infused with the eclectic artistic vitality and cultural vibrancy of our home in New Orleans.
summary: A boundary-breaking hub that explores and shares the complexity of the American Jewish Experience with scholars, students, and the public.

name: Tulane Med Wellness Committee
description:
Mission Statement:

The Tulane University School of Medicine Wellness Committee was created to coordinate student, resident, and faculty initiatives to create an environment that promotes well-being and builds a foundation for formal wellness programming.

Objectives:

  • Promote and increase visibility, utilization, and access to existing mental health and wellness services
  • Identify and address unmet needs
  • Recruit wellness champions among UME, GME, faculty and staff
  • Initiate new programing to promote mental health and well-being

Organization Structure:

The Tulane SOM Wellness committee is open to all faculty, staff, residents/fellows, and students within the Tulane School of Medicine. Three primary faculty (Geraldine Menard, Chrissy Guidry, and Karen Weissbecker) work together to lead and develop specific groups within the SOM: faculty and staff, residency/GME, and students. Wellness committee members are invited to participate and focus on any area or multiple areas of the committee. The committee reviews wellness initiatives, creates action plans, and tracks progress on wellness goals.
summary: The Tulane University School of Medicine Wellness Committee was created to coordinate student, resident, and faculty initiatives to create an environment that promotes well-being and builds a foundation for formal wellness programming.

name: Tulane Hospitality
description:
At Tulane Hospitality, powered by Aramark Collegiate Hospitality, we know the power of excellent service. As an industry leader with over 50 years of experience, we witness firsthand the continual evolution of the higher education landscape as standards and expectations are raised by the day. Which is why we seek to take our service a step further with each and every unique experience we craft.

We’re not simply serving quality food on a plate, we’re serving worlds brought together. We’re not just setting tables, we’re sparking dialogues. We’re not just partners, we’re ingrained in your unique identity and vision—with you every step of the way to create the best, most inspiring campus experience imaginable so you can stand out in the crowded higher education landscape. ​​​​​​​

Because when you set your sights and standards higher, you’re serving more than just good meals. You’re serving futures.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

name: Tulane University History Project
description:
The Tulane University History Project is a long-term effort to research and develop a detailed history of the University and its campuses, with respect to its racial history and founding, including the impacts from segregation and slavery.  The history project will encompass a deep and rigorous historical study of Tulane University.  It will cover the university’s founding as the Medical College of Louisiana in 1834 through the present day. The project will ultimately produce a nuanced, complex, and honest history of the university that engages its relationship with slavery, segregation and issues of racial equity.  
 
The Tulane University History Project, housed in the Office of Academic Affairs and Provost, will be in conversation with these broader university EDI efforts.  The project will acknowledge and build upon existing research and scholarship of faculty, staff, students, alumni and others who have engaged aspects of Tulane’s racial history.  It will also explore new questions and pathways of learning that aid in producing a chronological biography of Tulane’s racial history.

Taking an honest look at Tulane’s past is a necessary step in the university’s commitment to becoming a more equitable, diverse, and inclusive community. The Tulane University History Project will help Tulanians understand the institution’s past with an eye toward creating a guide to a better future.
summary: The Tulane University History Project is a long-term effort to research and develop a detailed history of the University and its campuses, with respect to its racial history and founding, including the impacts from segregation and slavery.

name: Tulane Law Library
description:
Mission: Tulane Law Library supports the research and curricular needs of the faculty and students of the law school by providing a collection of over 668,000 print materials, a wealth of electronic legal materials, and the highest possible level of service. To the extent possible, the Tulane Law Library also supports the research needs of the greater community as well as scholars from outside the Tulane community.

Non-Law TU Faculty, Staff, and Students: YES, you can check out materials from our library! Bring your Splash Card and ask our Circulation Desk for a circulation privileges application during public hours (M-F, 7:30am-7pm*). Applications are processed within 24 business hours (usually much less).

Public Hours vs. Law School Only Hours: During public hours, the Law Library is open to the TU community as well as the public.  During Law School Only Hours, the Law Library is only open to current Tulane Law students as well as current Tulane Law faculty and staff.     

Attending a Louisiana college or university, but not at Tulane? Ask your home institution's librarians about a "LOUIS Card" (formerly Lalinc) if you need to check out our materials! 

MORE INFO  

Tulane Law School is nestled amongst beautiful live oak trees and is within walking distance of the Audubon Park and Zoo in uptown New Orleans. The Law Library is located on floors three through six of Weinmann Hall. The collection of more than 684,000 volumes and volume equivalents includes standard Anglo-American legal materials and substantial collections of foreign, international, and comparative law. The Law Library's collection is especially rich in civil law material and houses one of the finest collections on maritime and admiralty law in the world. 

The Law Library is also home to the world-renowned Judge John Minor Wisdom Collection containing his judicial papers and personal library accumulated during his forty years on the US 5th Circuit Court of Appeals. Students, faculty and scholars from all over the world come to the Law Library and the Wisdom Collection to conduct research and engage in legal scholarship. Additionally, the Law Library contains a large US government documents collection.

Direct Link to our hours: 


*Please note that on Fridays during the summer and break as well as other selected Fridays during the regular semester we close at 5pm.
summary: Tulane Law Library supports the research and curricular needs of law school faculty and students. Additionally, TLL welcomes the entire Tulane University during our public hours, including access to materials. Entrance: 3rd Floor of Weinmann Hall.

description:
Planned Parenthood Generation Action Network is a network of young organizers and activists across the country who organize events on their campuses and in their communities to mobilize advocates for reproductive freedom; in addition to raising public awareness about reproductive health and rights, educating young people about sexual health, and creating lasting change in those communities.

Planned Parenthood Generation Action is committed to building a robust, national network of young leaders and arming them with the tools needed to create change through a range of national campaigns, conferences, and leadership opportunities.

This unique network acknowledges and supports the dynamic perspectives and intersectional framework that young people are using to connect reproductive health to other issue like civil rights, immigration, the environment, and identity politics.
