name: Latinas Unidas de Tulane University
description:
Latinas Unidas aims to build a strong, inclusive network at Tulane University, centered on cultural pride, heritage, and empowerment. Through our three core pillars—connection, community engagement, and cultural recognition—we will create a supportive space where members can create lasting relationships, engage with the Tulane and New Orleans community, and celebrate the rich diversity of Latine culture.

While our mission focuses on empowering women within the Latine community, we welcome individuals of all genders and backgrounds. Our organization will be a space where everyone feels valued and respected, without barriers to participation. We recognize that the Latina experience is diverse and multifaceted, and we will celebrate the unique perspectives that each member brings. Latinas Unidas will provide a platform for Latinas to come together, share their experiences, and challenge stereotypes, ultimately fostering unity and support within our community.

Our impact on campus will extend beyond the members of our organization. We aim to increase the visibility of Latinas at Tulane by establishing Latinas Unidas as a recognized and influential organization. By educating the Tulane community about Latine culture through holding events, and by fostering connections with other groups, we will strive to strengthen allyship and promote cultural understanding. Through our efforts, we hope to inspire Tulane to invest more in its Latina community, acknowledging the critical importance of cultural representation and support.

Latinas Unidas is not just for today; we aim to build a legacy that future generations can continue to shape, ensuring that the voices and experiences of Latinas will be amplified and celebrated on Tulane’s campus for years to come.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Latinas Unidas is established for the purpose of connecting Latinas and promoting cultural pride of Latin American heritage at Tulane University through our three pillars: connection, community engagement, and cultural recognition.

We at JOC Tulane offer a safe and supportive space for Jewish students to be able to report antisemitic activity on campus or talk about any situation that made them feel discriminated against or uncomfortable. It has been increasingly obviously as of recent that a need for more Jewish advocacy is crucial for maintaining the safety and equality of students at Tulane. Our goal is to create a streamlined awareness service for students at Tulane (as being done with other chapters nationwide) so that Jewish voices are heard, represented, empowered; not just on our campus but on campus all across the nation.  

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

description:
Hopelessly Yellow is a digital brand promoting mental wellness and positivity through writing, design, music, and media. Hopelessly Yellow strives to foster a passionate community for all. More than just a club, Hopelessly Yellow embodies a blissful feeling, a fresh mindset, and a golden lifestyle.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

name: Feminism, Redefined
description:
• Unsatisfied with the intellect of modern feminism? Desperate for open-minded political conversations? Just looking for a space to rant about hookup culture? Join Feminism, Redefined! Feminism, Redefined is a bipartisan discussion group committed to open-minded exploration of various topics relating to gender and sex including hookup culture, pornography, sex work and how feminism affects men. Feminism, Redefined is a feminist group because of its belief in gender equality, but its discussions will question modern feminist dogmas and members will be challenged to explore topics outside the bounds of ideology. This group aims to re-examine and ultimately redefine the objectives of feminism to better serve the people the movement is fighting for. Students of all political beliefs (including non-feminists!), genders and backgrounds are encouraged to join anytime. 

    Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: A bipartisan discussion group committed to open-minded exploration of various topics relating to gender and sex including hookup culture, pornography, and how feminism affects men.

name: Data Analytics and Science Hub
description:
The Data Analytics and Science Hub (DASH) at Tulane University aims to foster a collaborative environment including students from diverse backgrounds: data science, medicine, business, and other fields. Together, we can explore and develop innovative ideas that address challenges that influencing health disparities. As a research-driven student organization, DASH leverages publicly available databases to provide education opportunities, practical training, and networking opportunities. With access to Tulane's extensive resources, we seek to connect teams of researchers across various disciplines. The DASH e-board members represent both the uptown and downtown campuses, including the School of Biomedical Engineering, School of Medicine, and School of Public Health. Our goal is to provide students with hands-on experience while ensuring equitable outcomes through debiasing and enhanced collaboration across departments. Our goal is to produce scientific publications, participating in hackathon, engaging case competitions that contribute to the understanding and solving critical issues that impact health outcome of our community.  

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

name: Brain Exercise Initiative
description:
The Brain Exercise Initiative is a completely student led 501(c)(3)nonprofit organization. It was originally founded by UCLA student Esin Gumustekin, who was inspired by research done in Japan by neuroscientist Dr. Ryuta Kawashima. Dr. Kawashima found that those with Alzheimer’s showed great improvements by doing simple math and reading exercises for 30 minutes a day, 5 times a week. The improvements were found in cognitive functioning (measured by the Mini Mental State Examinations and the Frontal Assessment Batteries), feelings of happiness, and communication. Many individuals showed improvements in communication and behavior. There were even some miraculous cases, such as a bedridden individual who began to walk again after completing the exercises for 6 months. While these activities are regularly practiced in care centers in Japan, Esin was inspired to make these activities more commonplace in other countries as well. Thus, Brain Exercise Initiative was born: an intergenerational nonprofit where university students conduct brain exercise programs in retirement homes. 

 

Currently, our organization BEI has chapters at 65 universities, including UCLA, Columbia, Cornell, Notre Dame, Brown, and numerous others. These sessions at the nursing/retirement  homes will consist of simple math (addition, subtraction, etc), reading aloud, and writing exercises as well as partaking in other fun games bringing our youthful spirits.
summary: The Brain Exercise Initiative at Tulane University uses math, reading, and writing as a form of cognitive therapy. Research has found that exercises for 30 minutes a day can positively impact cognitive function for those with Alzheimer's disease.

name: Tulane Triathlon and Ultramarathon club
description:
• At the Iron Wave, we’re dedicated to empowering individuals to reach their potential in endurance sports, from Ironman triathlons to ultramarathons. Our mission is to guide athletes of all levels through comprehensive training and education, fostering the skills and resilience needed to succeed in some of the world’s most challenging races. We believe that everyone, regardless of background or experience, should have the opportunity to discover the life-changing power of endurance sports.
  • Led by a passionate team of seasoned triathletes, runners, swimmers, and cyclists, we share not only expertise but also a profound love for these sports. Whether you’re a seasoned competitor aiming for new milestones or a beginner inspired to take on your first event, our community offers the support, encouragement, and guidance to help you reach your goals.
  • Join us to find your stride, build lasting friendships, and ignite your passion for swimming, biking, and running—one mile at a time. Together, we’ll push boundaries, inspire growth, and celebrate every success along the way.
  • Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Iron Wave is an organization that is dedicated to supporting Tulane students in endurance sports and Iron Mans. The organization will provide opportunities for personal growth and an inclusive, community styled training environment.

We also plan on having events such as a charity walkathon, specific themed walks (that we will vote on later in the semester!), and a club-dinner. This is a zero-commitment, stress-free club so you get out what you put in! name: Value in Virtual Access Tulane
description:
VIVA Tulane (Value in Virtual Access) is a student-led organization dedicated to enhancing digital literacy and maximizing the benefits of Tulane University’s extensive technology offerings. The university invests heavily in providing students with access to cutting-edge software suites, cloud services, and innovative digital tools—yet many students remain unaware of, or underutilize, these valuable resources. VIVA Tulane seeks to bridge that gap by hosting workshops, seminars, and collaborative events that help students explore the full potential of these digital perks. From familiar platforms like Adobe’s Creative Cloud and Box cloud storage to specialized software such as ArcGIS, the club highlights how each tool can support academic success, career readiness, and personal projects.

In addition to boosting tech-savviness on campus, VIVA Tulane also serves as a liaison between the Tulane community and leading tech providers. University subscription versions of popular software often feature unique login methods, access restrictions, or missing documentation compared to standard retail editions—differences that can introduce unexpected bugs or make troubleshooting more complex. Online resources typically cater to retail users, meaning student-license quirks aren’t always addressed. By gathering and reporting issues directly to technology companies and proposing new features informed by student feedback, VIVA Tulane ensures the campus community’s voice is heard. Ultimately, the club fosters a collaborative ecosystem where students, faculty, and industry partners work together to continually refine and improve the digital tools that power learning and creativity at Tulane and beyond.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Student-led initiative aimed at promoting digital literacy and making the most of Tulane's extensive technology resources. The club raises awareness and impact of cutting-edge software, cloud services, and digital perks provided by the university.

name: Tulane Entrepreneurship Club
description:
The Tulane Entrepreneurship Club is a student-driven organization dedicated to empowering the next generation of business leaders and innovators at Tulane University. We create a dynamic environment where entrepreneurial-minded students connect, learn, and grow together while developing essential skills and networks for success in the startup world.

Our initiatives include monthly speaker series featuring successful entrepreneurs, hands-on workshops covering startup fundamentals, networking events with New Orleans' vibrant business community, and mentorship opportunities. Through our programming, members gain practical experience, valuable connections, and the chance to participate in pitch competitions where they can present their ideas and receive feedback.

We welcome students from all academic backgrounds – from business and engineering to liberal arts and sciences – creating an interdisciplinary environment that sparks innovation. As part of New Orleans' growing startup ecosystem, we maintain strong partnerships with local accelerators, incubators, and business leaders, providing our members unique insights and opportunities in entrepreneurship.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Tulane Entrepreneurship Club empowers students to transform ideas into reality by connecting them with entrepreneurs, resources, and opportunities. We foster a community of ambitious student leaders passionate about building ventures.

summary: JGOT builds a vibrant community for Jewish grad and professional students at Tulane, connecting peers across programs. We celebrate Jewish culture, offer social, cultural, and professional opportunities, promote interfaith dialogue, and support students.

name: Alpha Alpha Alpha
description:
Alpha Alpha Alpha (Tri Alpha) is a national honor society dedicated to recognizing and celebrating the academic achievements of first-generation college students. Founded in 2018, Tri Alpha promotes academic excellence, leadership, and community engagement among students who are the first in their families to earn a four-year degree. Membership in Tri Alpha provides students with opportunities for networking, mentorship, and professional development while fostering a supportive community of scholars. The Lambda Sigma Chapter at Tulane University seeks to uphold these values by recognizing outstanding first-generation students, supporting their academic and personal success, and enhancing their collegiate experience through service and leadership initiatives.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

The Tulane Undergraduate Law Review (TULR) provides Tulane undergraduate students with a platform to critically analyze and engage with key legal issues while developing essential skills in legal research, writing, and argumentation. As a student-run, student-written, and peer-reviewed scholarly journal, TULR is committed to fostering intellectual discourse and publishing high-quality legal scholarship on a semi-annual basis.

The TULR aims to bridge the gap between undergraduate legal inquiry and the broader legal community by addressing pressing legal topics relevant to students and scholars alike.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The TULR is an entirely student-run, student-written, and peer-reviewed scholarly journal, focusing on pressing legal issues presented at the undergraduate level for the Tulane University and legal community.

name: Council for Global Health Scholars
description:
The Council of Global Health Scholars at Tulane is a post secondary accredited institution that brings together students who are passionate about global health challenges. It’s a space where members can connect with like-minded peers, learn from experts, and take action on issues like health inequities, infectious diseases, and access to care.

The chapter organizes hands-on workshops, guest lectures, and networking events to help students grow academically and professionally. Members can get involved in research projects, community outreach programs, and even international service opportunities, gaining real-world experience and a deeper understanding of global health systems.

Beyond events, the chapter emphasizes mentorship and peer support, helping members develop leadership skills and explore career paths in medicine, public health, or global health policy. Members have the opportunity of raising awareness and working on initiatives that show how global health issues affect communities close to home.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The CGHS at Tulane connects students passionate about global health through workshops, research, outreach, and mentorship. It empowers members to tackle health inequities, gain real-world experience, and develop leadership skills for impactful careers.

name: Center for Health Outcomes, Implementation, & Community-Engaged Science
description: The Center for Health Outcomes, Implementation & Community Engaged Science (CHOICES) is supported in part by the Jack Aron Chair in Primary Care Medicine and under the direction of Dr. Marie Krousel-Wood. The Center’s vision is to improve health outcomes, achieve health equity, and increase access and quality of primary and preventive care in Louisiana, the Gulf South, and beyond. CHOICES is accomplishing its goals by conducting community-engaged, interdisciplinary, investigator-initiated, extramurally funded clinical, translational, and population-based research; broadly disseminating researching findings; and fostering the career development of the next generation of research scholars. Our work supports our communities in Louisiana and neighboring Gulf South areas through community-engaged research addressing pressing clinical and public health problems to support health equity, fostering the career development of students/ residents/faculty pursuing academic and research careers, and increasing knowledge and awareness of primary care and preventive medicine research.
summary: CHOICES aims to improve health outcomes, achieve health equity, and increase access and quality of primary and preventive care in Louisiana by supporting communities through community-engaged research addressing clinical and public health problems.

name: John Lewis Public Administration Program
description:
The John Lewis Public Administration Program at Tulane SoPA offers a Master of Public Administration (MPA) degree that will prepare you to lead in the field and create thriving communities by addressing the critical societal challenges.  Our faculty bring decades of on-the-ground, impactful work throughout the local and national community to the classroom.

Whether you're interested in public infrastructure and governance, nonprofit leadership, public/private partnerships, or data analytics, an MPA from Tulane SoPA can help you pursue whichever path you choose. With your MPA, you’ll be ready to apply for various public service leadership positions, including:

  • Local, state, or federal government manager
  • Nonprofit or philanthropic organization executive
  • Government, nonprofit, or private consulting firm policy research analyst
  • Program evaluator in government, nonprofits, or the private sector
  • Government or private sector data manager
  • Public finance manager or analyst
  • Corporate responsibility manager
  • Public affairs specialist

Click here to find out how our online MPA program can help you strengthen your community and reach your career goals.
summary: Are you interested in a career in public service?   Tulane SoPA's John Lewis Public Administration Program offers a Master of Public Administration (MPA) degree that prepares you to be a leader ready to tackle challenges and create thriving communities

name: Omega Psi Phi Fraternity Inc.
description:
Omega Psi Phi Fraternity, Inc. is a historically African American fraternity founded on November 17, 1911, at Howard University in Washington, D.C. by four undergraduate students—Frank Coleman, Oscar J. Cooper, Edgar A. Love, and their faculty advisor Ernest E. Just. It was the first international fraternal organization founded at a historically Black college.

Guided by the cardinal principles of Manhood, Scholarship, Perseverance, and Uplift, Omega Psi Phi promotes academic excellence, community service, and leadership development. Its motto, "Friendship is Essential to the Soul," emphasizes the value of strong brotherhood.

The fraternity has initiated over 750 chapters globally, including undergraduate and graduate chapters, and has a strong legacy of service, particularly in mentoring, health initiatives, and social justice advocacy. Notable members include Michael Jordan, Langston Hughes, Shaquille O’Neal, and Jesse Jackson.

Omega Psi Phi also organizes national programs such as Achievement Week, Scholarship programs, and social action projects, reflecting its deep commitment to uplifting the African American community. The organization continues to play a vital role in shaping Black leadership and advancing civil rights.
summary: Omega Psi Phi Fraternity, Inc. is a historically Black fraternity founded in 1911 at Howard University. Guided by the principles of manhood, scholarship, perseverance, and uplift, it promotes academic excellence and community service.

name: Corporation and Foundations Relations
description:
The Corporate and Foundation Relations team collaborates with you to draft your proposal. Because you know your work best, we ask you to provide the technical context of the proposal and we will work with you to ensure your draft is prepared according to the funder's priorities and interests.

The Corporate and Foundation Relations team cultivates relationships with corporations and foundations that advance priority initiatives and generate financial support across Tulane University. To improve opportunities to secure funding, our team draws on its experiences with corporations and foundations and utilizes a range of services and resources across campus to ensure that proposals are optimally positioned for success.

These working relationships are key to maintaining the internal communication that is essential for upholding Tulane's reputation of excellence with external funders. This is one of the main reasons our office encourages all faculty and staff to contact us first when considering approaching a foundation.
summary: We work with university leaders to identify and raise funding for school priorities. We assist Tulane faculty and staff in drawing private external funding for programs and research.

name: Abide College Fellowship
description: Rooted in Christ, Abiding in Truth, Fruitful in Faith. ACF is the college ministry of Church of the Resurrection, which is within walking distance to Tulane's campus. The mission of Abide College Fellowship is to glorify God by keeping the Gospel central to all we do, as we live out and proclaim the good news of Jesus Christ (1 Corinthians 2:2; 15:3-4). We are committed to teaching others to walk in the way of Jesus, making disciples who faithfully follow Him (Matthew 28:18-19). Through the prioritization of God’s Word, both in individual study and as a community, we seek to grow in wisdom, faith, and obedience (2 Timothy 3:16-17; Matthew 4:4; Romans 10:17; Hebrews 4:12). We strive to build deep, authentic community, united by our love for Jesus and one another, encouraging each other toward love and good deeds (Hebrews 10:24-25; Ecclesiastes 4:9-12; Galatians 6:2; Romans 1:12).
summary: Abide College Fellowship seeks to create an environment for spiritual growth and character formation through the study and application of God’s Word. We aim to glorify God in our conduct, academic pursuits, and relationships with others.

name: The National Society of Leadership and Success
description:
The National Society of Leadership and Success (NSLS) is the nation’s largest leadership honor society, dedicated to building leaders who make a better world. Our chapter at Tulane University will provide students with opportunities to develop essential leadership skills, build professional networks, and engage in meaningful service to the campus and community.

Through speaker broadcasts, leadership training, peer networking, and community service, members gain practical tools for personal and professional growth. Our organization is committed to empowering students to set and achieve their goals, foster a culture of collaboration, and prepare members to be successful leaders in their future careers and communities. We welcome students of all backgrounds, identities, and experiences, and are dedicated to creating an inclusive and supportive environment where every member feels valued, respected, and inspired to reach their full potential.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The National Society of Leadership and Success is an organization that provides a life-changing leadership program that helps students achieve personal growth, career success, and empowers them to have a positive impact in their community and beyond.

We establish our travel team at the beginning of each new season, and allow all those who do not make the team to attend practices as practice players. We practice every Tuesday and Thursday from 6-8pm in the Reily Recreation Center Auxiliary Gym. The travel team travels to tournaments across the south throughout the season, and attends NCVF nationals in April.
summary: Tulane Men's Club Volleyball competes as a part of the NCVF and the SIVA conference. We practice every Tuesday and Thursday from 6-8pm. We travel to tournaments across the south throughout the season, and attend NCVF nationals in April.

Leaders and practitioners driving public health impact to ensure optimal health and wellbeing in local and global communities.
summary: The Office of Public Health Practice serves to support the CSW SPHTM community in practice opportunities and linkages within the school and externally.

name: Performing Arts at Tulane
description:
Experience. Create. Engage with us.
Performing Arts at Tulane invites all members of our community—students, faculty, staff, and the broader New Orleans region—to explore, imagine, and perform. Uniting Theatre, Dance, Music, and Musical Theatre, we foster artistic curiosity, rigorous scholarship, and rooted cultural expression.
We champion inclusive artistic excellence, grounded in Tulane’s rich liberal arts tradition and New Orleans’s extraordinary cultural tapestry. Our programs are committed to creating new work, preserving heritage, and partnering locally and globally. Through performance, research, and shared creative practice, we shape artists and thinkers who lead with imagination, integrity, and inspired purpose.

summary: The Tulane Recovery Community’s (TRC) mission is to support individualized recovery on campus by providing recovery-based programs that foster academic achievement, connection, and engagement.

summary: Tenenbaum End-Of-Year Celebration

name: Staff Advisory Council
description: The Staff Advisory Council is officially recognized by the university administration as representing the views of staff employees. SAC holds seats on the University Senate and several Senate subcommittees, including Faculty & Staff Benefits and Budget Review.
summary: The Staff Advisory Council is a university-wide body of elected and appointed staff employees.

name: American Medical Association (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane AMA chapter is a student-led organization dedicated to cultivating future physician-leaders, advocating for quality patient care, and promoting medical professionalism. As part of the the national American Medical Association, our chapter strives to be a bridge between medical students, faculty, and the larger medical community, fostering dialogue on healthcare policy, ethics, and clinical innovation.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The AMA's mission is to promote the art and science of medicine and the betterment of public health. The Medical Student Section (AMA-MSS) strives to be medical students' voice within the AMA for improving medical education.

name: American Medical Student Association (Graduate - MSG)
description:
An organization dedicated to preparing the next generation of doctors to be leaders and advocates. We champion quality, affordable, and equitable healthcare for all, guiding medical students to create a more accessible healthcare.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Goal of AMSA: Keep student body informed on health policy issues through presentations and speakers, help students learn more about health policies!

name: American Medical Women's Association (Graduate - MSG)
description:
Tulane's chapter of the American Medical Women's Association (AMWA) functions as an extension of the national organization, working to empower women in medicine and address issues involving women's health by organizing various events and initiatives.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The mission of the American Medical Women's Association (AMWA) is to advance women physicians and improve the health of women.

name: American Physician Scientist Association (Graduate - MSG)
description:
American Physician Scientists Association, APSA, is an extension of the National APSA Chapter, with the purpose of providing a platform for medical students to integrate research, connect, and be supported with structural opportunities to pursue research.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: We are the Tulane Medical School Chapter of the national American Physician Scientists Association.

name: C. Thorpe Ray Internal Medicine Society (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The C. Thorpe Ray Internal Medicine Society is dedicated to promoting the ideals and interests of the field of Internal Medicine through both social and academic functions. Participation in this organization provides medical students with additional opportunities to pursue their interests and increase their knowledge in the field of Internal Medicine. Group functions also provide students with the opportunity to interact with the Internal Medicine faculty and residents on an informal basis. The CTR IM Society’s events cover a broad range of topics that allow for learning and discussion regarding the field of Internal Medicine. Through participation and attendance at CTR events, students can begin to explore and navigate the field of Internal Medicine to prepare for their clinical experiences and beyond. CTR hosts a Residency Match Panel in the Spring, meant to inform and help guide 3rd year students in preparing for the Interview and Match process. One of the social events hosted by the C. Thorpe Ray IM Society is the Annual Fall Mixer, intended to encourage connection and networking between medical students interested in Internal Medicine, current IM residents, and IM faculty, in a casual setting. The organization also puts on the “Crawfish Dash” in conjunction with the Tulane Running Club. The Crawfish Dash is a 5K run, held in Audubon Park, with crawfish waiting at the finish line for runners to re-fuel and hang out after they finish.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The C. Thorpe Ray Internal Medicine Society is dedicated to promoting the ideals and interests of the field of Internal Medicine through both social and academic functions. Participation in this organization provides medical students with additional...

name: Culinary Medicine Society (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane University Culinary Medicine Society (CMS) is a student-run organization partnered with the Goldring Center for Culinary Medicine (GCCM). CMS provides medical students with hands-on learning in nutrition and cooking by staffing teaching kitchens, organizing student-led workshops, and engaging in community outreach programs. The society promotes the integration of evidence-based nutrition into medical training and patient care while also fostering collaboration between students, chefs, and healthcare professionals. Through its activities, CMS equips future physicians with the skills to counsel patients on healthy eating and lifestyle strategies that support disease prevention and overall wellness.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Tulane Culinary Medicine Society (CMS), within the Goldring Center for Culinary Medicine, gives medical students hands-on experience through community outreach, teaching kitchens, and student events focused on nutrition and health.

name: Dermatology Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane Dermatology Interest Group, DIG, provides students with the opportunity to learn more about the field of dermatology and gives them the chance to apply their growing knowledge in a practical setting. We sponsor lectures on the field of general dermatology and the sub-specialties within dermatology, and match panels with 4th year students and residents. We also sponsor a service learning project, SPOTS (Sun Protection Outreach Teaching to Students), in order to educate teens at local middle and high schools about the dangers of tanning, the warning signs of skin cancer, and methods for sun protection. The club provides a forum for medical students who would like to match in dermatology to meet other students, residents, and dermatologists.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Tulane Dermatology Interest Group, DIG, provides students with the opportunity to learn more about the field of dermatology and gives them the chance to apply their growing knowledge in a practical setting.

name: Garden Club (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane School of Medicine Garden Club was formed to create opportunities for community members to engage in gardening activities while promoting health and wellness within both the TUSOM community and the greater New Orleans area. The club serves as a welcoming space for plant enthusiasts, whether amateurs or professional gardeners, to share their botanical interests and knowledge of herbal medicine. Through its initiatives, the Garden Club aims to improve the environmental health of the community, increase access to outdoor spaces in urban settings, and provide educational opportunities about plants, food, and sustainable gardening practices.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Garden Club promotes health, wellness, and environmental sustainability by bringing people together to garden, share botanical interests, and expand access to urban green spaces.

name: Latino Medical Student Association (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Latino Medical Student Association (LMSA) at Tulane School of Medicine is dedicated to uniting and empowering Latino/a/x medical students, while advocating for the health and well-being of Latino communities in New Orleans and beyond. As a chapter of the national LMSA organization, we strive to create a supportive network that fosters academic success, leadership development, mentorship, and service opportunities. Our members are passionate about addressing health disparities, promoting diversity in medicine, and advancing culturally competent care for underserved populations. Through community engagement, outreach programs, and professional development initiatives, we aim to inspire the next generation of Latino physicians and allies committed to equity and social justice in healthcare. We collaborate with local organizations, faculty mentors, and fellow student groups to host events ranging from health fairs and service projects to educational workshops and cultural celebrations. At Tulane, our chapter provides a space where students can find community, celebrate their heritage, and grow as future leaders in medicine.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity
summary: The Latino Medical Student Association (LMSA) at Tulane School of Medicine brings together Latino/a/x medical students and allies to promote diversity in medicine, support student success, and advocate for the health of underserved communities.

name: Legacy Donor Operation Donation Club (Graduate - MSG)
description: The foremost purpose of the Legacy Donor Operation Donation Club is to increase awareness and help future doctors be aware of facts and the importance of organ donation with an association with a local nonprofit: Legacy Donor Foundation in partnership with Donate Life Louisiana. As a club, we will work to spread awareness about the process of organ donation, dispel myths and misconceptions surrounding the process, and stress the importance of becoming an organ donor.
summary: Legacy Donor Operation Donation Club aims to increase awareness and help future doctors be aware of facts and the importance of organ donation

name: Let's PLAY (Graduate - MSG)
description:
Let’s PLAY organizes weekly, 2-hour sessions led by organization leads and medical student volunteers at a local elementary school. These sessions are divided into three separate parts: interactive teaching sessions, physical activity-based recreation, and a post-activity discussion. Teaching sessions will be aimed at physical health (exercise, diet, etc) and mental health. Activity based recreation will be games, sports, etc. Post activity discussion will be a quick summary to clear up and confusion and goals before the next session

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Let’s PLAY is a non-profit organization that promotes healthy and active lifestyles in elementary schools around the country. As a volunteer, you will work alongside Tulane athletes as coaches, players, and mentors in sports.

name: Lions Club (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane School of Medicine Lions Club chapter is dedicated to making a difference in the New Orleans community and beyond! This passionate group of medical students and professionals works alongside Lions Clubs International to tackle vision-related issues, offer health services, and support local outreach initiatives. Through the “CubSight” Program, we host visual screenings at local schools using state-of-the-art screening equipment. During the screenings, we identify children with possible vision disorders, inform their guardians, and refer them to a licensed vision professional for a comprehensive eye exam. Through the “Sight For Kids” program, we host pop-up mobile clinics at schools that have been screened by us previously. Our mobile clinic allows us to connect children who are in need of care with a physician for treatment. During these clinics, the physician will perform comprehensive dilated eye exams and provide glasses prescriptions. Once the children visit with the physician, an optician on-site gathers their required measurements and helps them pick out two pairs of frames. The program “Spare Pair” provides these children with two free pairs of glasses, one for the child to take home and one to keep in their classroom. Once their glasses arrive, we return to the school to dispense and adjust the child’s new glasses. We also host visual screenings for adults at free local health fairs throughout the greater New Orleans area in partnership with local health clinics.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Tulane Lions Club partners closely with the Louisiana Lions Eye Foundation (LLEF) to administer free visual screenings to Louisiana residents. Our mission is to improve our community’s quality of life through vision care.

name: Medical Genetics Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The TUSOM Medical Genetics Interest Group exposes students to the field of medical genetics and aims to create opportunities for students to develop and explore their interests related to genetics. Our group provides a forum for the discussion of topics related to genetics as a medical specialty and serves as an information resource and mentoring body for individuals interested in genetics.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: MGIG introduces students to the field of medical genetics, fostering discussion and mentorship for those interested in pursuing genetics.

name: Med-Peds Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Med-Peds clinic is a resident run clinic where patients around New Orleans come for a variety of concerns. Our medical students shadow at the clinic for experience and service learning hours. Shifts are Tuesdays and Wednesdays from 1-4pm. Students may take patient histories on their own if they feel comfortable. They then follow the resident as they present the case to the attending. The club also hosts events to spread awareness about different diseases every few months. For example, Med-Peds Interest Group will be hosting a fundraiser for the families affected by breast cancer this October.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Connect medical students with the resident run Med-Peds Clinic at 1901 Tulane Ave. Students have the opportunity to shadow residents as they meet with patients.

name: Music & Medicine (Graduate - MSG)
description:
Music and Medicine is a student-ran club full of musicians throughout all four years of medical school. We frequently place together and also perform at various events. Events largely include service learning activities, the annual Jambalaya, and different medical school functions. All skill levels are welcomed.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all medical school students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Music and Medicine is a student-ran club full of musicians throughout all four years of medical school.

name: Orthopedic Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Orthopedic Surgery Interest Group is a student-run organization that introduces medical students to the field of orthopedics. We host lunch talks with faculty and residents, provide opportunities for students to learn about orthopedic research and clinical experiences, and connect interested students with mentors and activities in the orthopedic community. 

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Club for those interested in orthopedics.

name: Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation (PM&R) Interest Group is a student-run organization dedicated to exposing medical students to the field of PM&R. Our goal is to increase awareness of PM&R through hosting various events, including introductory meetings, open-ended talks with current physicians, and match panels with current medical students that have matched into a PM&R residency. We hope to provide students with positive networking experiences and several opportunities in research and mentoring.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation (PM&R) Interest Group is an organization dedicated to exposing medical students to the field of PM&R. We hope to provide students with positive networking experiences and several opportunities in research.

name: Psychiatry Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Psychiatry Interest Group is a student-led organization dedicated to promoting awareness, education, and interest in the field of psychiatry. Our group provides opportunities for medical students to explore the diverse subspecialties within psychiatry. Through guest lectures, networking, research involvement, and community outreach, we aim to foster both academic and professional development for students considering careers in psychiatry. In addition to supporting career exploration, PsychIG is committed to addressing the broader issues of mental health and wellness. We work closely with faculty mentors and local organizations to host events that highlight mental health advocacy, stigma reduction, and culturally competent care for underserved populations. By engaging in service opportunities such as the annual NAMI Walk, mental health awareness campaigns, and collaborative projects, our members actively contribute to improving access and equity in mental healthcare. Ultimately, the Psychiatry Interest Group strives to create a supportive community for students while encouraging a deeper understanding of psychiatry’s vital role in medicine.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Psychiatry Interest Group at Tulane School of Medicine introduces students to the field of psychiatry through mentorship, educational events, and community building. Our mission is to foster interest in mental health, reduce stigma, and support...

name: Radiation Oncology Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Radiation Oncology Interest Group (ROIG) is a student run organization dedicated to promoting exposure of TUSOM medical students to the field of radiation oncology. We aim to accomplish this through expert talks and clinical experiences with board-certified radiation oncologists in the NOLA community. Radiation Oncology is the use of radiation therapy to treat cancer. At this time, the state of Louisiana lacks any radiation oncology residencies which puts students at a disadvantage at gaining relevant experiences and matching into the field. TUSOM currently has two Radiation Oncologists, and ROIG forms liaisons between TUSOM students and local radiation oncologists. ROIG plans to promote exposure of TUSOM medical students to the field of radiation oncology by coordinating at least two events per semester such as physician speakers, expert panel collaboration with the medical oncology and surgical oncology interest groups, tours to see cutting edge radiation therapy equipment, trips to observe tumor boards, expert talks on covering interesting cases, and collaboration with other groups. Radiation oncology is a very unique and specific field which requires matching into its own residency program. Through the coordinated events, ROIG aims to provide Tulane medical students with opportunities to explore this exciting field.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all (X School) students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Radiation Oncology Interest Group (ROIG) is a student run organization dedicated to promoting exposure of TUSOM medical students to the field of radiation oncology. We aim to accomplish this through expert talks and clinical experiences with...

name: Social Bike Club (Graduate - MSG)
description:
This club was founded to create a community of people – a krewe if you will – who enjoy biking, exploring, and having fun! We are open to all and believe in the philosophy “the more the merrier!” Here in New Orleans we like to say, “laissez le bon temps rouler,” let the good times roll! This club takes this saying to heart as we literally have a great time rolling around the city on our bikes! We have monthly rides to various locations around the city. The rides go at a leisurely pace and include music, lights, and stops at various destinations around the city. We encourage all participants to purchase lights for their bikes so that our krewe shines bright, both for safety and fun!

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: We hold group bike rides, bike tuneups, and other social activities related to biking for Tulane University School of Medicine.

name: Tulane International Medical Students (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane International Medical Students Association (TIMS) at our medical school is a student-led organization dedicated to supporting and connecting international medical students. The club provides a welcoming community that helps students adjust to life in a new academic and cultural environment while fostering friendships and mentorship. TIMS hosts social events, cultural celebrations, and academic support activities to encourage inclusivity and collaboration. By creating a supportive network, the association ensures international students feel at home while excelling in their medical training. TIMS also promotes cultural awareness among the wider student body, enriching the school’s diversity and global perspective.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Tulane International Medical Students Association (TIMS) at our medical school is a student-led organization dedicated to supporting and connecting international medical students.

name: Tulane Medical Ping Pong Club (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane Medical Ping Pong Club is a student-led organization dedicated to promoting wellness, stress relief, and community through table tennis. Our club welcomes players of all skill levels, from complete beginners to seasoned competitors. We provide a fun, inclusive space where students can take a break from the demands of medical school, connect with classmates across all years, and enjoy both casual and competitive play. Regular events include open play sessions, friendly tournaments, and opportunities to learn or improve skills. Beyond the game, the club fosters camaraderie, teamwork, and balance—values essential to thriving in medical school and beyond. Whether you’re looking to sharpen your reflexes, pick up a new hobby, or just have fun with friends, the Ping Pong Club is the perfect place to unwind and get involved.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Tulane Medical Ping Pong Club is a student-run organization that brings together all levels of players for casual games, friendly competition, and stress relief while fostering community across Tulane’s medical school.

name: Tulane University School of Medicine Running for Health Club (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Tulane University School of Medicine Run Club is a student-led organization dedicated to promoting health, wellness, and community through running. We host weekly runs across scenic New Orleans routes, organize student participation in major local races, and collaborate with other clubs to support charity events like St. Baldrick’s Foundation fundraisers. Beyond fitness, our club fosters connection among students, offering a supportive space to set and achieve personal goals. We are also proud to partner with Zeus’ Rescues, giving students the chance to earn service learning hours by running with shelter dogs. Whether you’re a seasoned runner or just starting out, TUSOM Run Club brings people together to stay active, support one another, and represent our medical school in the community.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: TUSOM Run Club builds community through fitness, hosting weekly runs, citywide races, and charity events. We collaborate with other student groups and even offer service learning hours for running with shelter dogs at Zeus’ Rescues.

name: Tulane Trauma Research Organization (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Trauma Research Interest Group (TRIG) at Tulane University School of Medicine promotes student involvement in trauma care, research, and injury prevention. The group connects students with faculty-led projects in trauma surgery and related subspecialties, while offering mentorship and guidance for both new and experienced researchers. TRIG holds regular meetings, participates in the weekly McSwain Trauma Research Meeting, and organizes lectures and workshops on topics such as IRB submission, research design, and advances in trauma care. Through hands-on collaboration with faculty and residents, TRIG encourages student-led research, fosters inter-interest group partnerships, and supports quality improvement initiatives. The group also extends its impact to the Greater New Orleans community by organizing service projects focused on health, wellness, and injury prevention. Together, these efforts create opportunities for mentorship, education, and leadership in the field of trauma.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Trauma Research Interest Group (TRIG) at Tulane School of Medicine fosters student engagement in trauma-related research, mentorship, and education. The group connects students with faculty-led projects, provides training in the research process...

name: Urology Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Urology Interest Group is a student-led organization dedicated to fostering interest and education in the field of urology. We provide opportunities for medical students to learn about urological diseases, surgical techniques, and cutting-edge research through lectures, workshops, and networking events with faculty, residents, and professionals in the field. Our goal is to engage and inspire future urologists by offering a platform for learning, mentorship, and career exploration.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The Urology Interest Group is a student-led organization dedicated to fostering interest and education in the field of urology. We provide opportunities for medical students to learn about urological diseases, surgical techniques, and cutting-edge...

name: Vascular Surgery Interest Group (Graduate - MSG)
description:
The Vascular Surgery Interest Group (VSIG) is a student-led organization dedicated to promoting awareness, education, and mentorship in the field of vascular surgery. Our mission is to expose students to the diverse clinical, academic, and research opportunities within vascular surgery while fostering professional development and early mentorship connections. VSIG hosts educational events, skills workshops, research discussions, and networking sessions with faculty and residents. Through these initiatives, we aim to cultivate curiosity, support career exploration, and inspire the next generation of vascular surgeons.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Interest Group for Vascular Surgery Enthusiasts!

name: Volleyball Club at TUSOM (Graduate - MSG)
description:
Tulane School of Medicine Volleyball Club is a student-run organization dedicated to fostering wellness, teamwork, and community through the sport of volleyball. Our club welcomes players of all skill levels, from beginners to experienced athletes, providing a fun and inclusive environment for everyone. We host regular practices, recreational games, and friendly competitions through our intramural team(s) to encourage fitness and stress relief outside of the classroom. Beyond athletics, the club serves as a social outlet for medical students to build connections and promote balance in the demanding environment of medical school. By engaging in both casual play and organized events, we strive to support wellness, teamwork, and school spirit within the TUSOM community.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all Medical School students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Tulane School of Medicine Volleyball Club brings students together for fun, fitness, and friendly competition. Open to all skill levels, we promote teamwork, stress relief, and community through regular games, practices, events, and intramurals.

name: Structural and Cellular Biology
description: The department includes 12 full-time faculty members, a number of adjunct faculty, and a support staff which combine to meet its educational and research goals. Educational responsibilities include teaching major components of the first-year medical school curriculum, including Gross & Developmental Anatomy, Human Medical Histology and Medical Neuroscience, as well as teaching various related graduate level courses including our Anatomy Certification Program and Masters Degree Programs. Research areas of expertise include reproductive endocrinology and toxicity, neuroendocinology, neuroendocine influences of breast cancer, endocrine response mechanisms of breast cancer and prostate cancer, circadian biology and circadian disruption in cancer, transposable elements and genetic instability in cancer, mechanisms of initiation, progression and metastasis of prostate cancer, and experimental therapeutics for breast and prostate cancer.
summary:
Department of Structural and Cellular Biology 

The Department of Structural and Cellular Biology (SCB) is committed to providing a quality education for both medical and graduate students while pursing funded research.

name: Summer Lyric Theatre at Tulane University
description:
Summer Lyric Theatre is a musical theatre performing organization based on the campus of Tulane University. Summer Lyric's mission is to preserve one of America's greatest art forms by producing the highest quality musical theatre. Our theatre exists to support and expand musicians, actors, singers, dancers, technical artists and most important, students of promise, as well as those whom are already established by bringing together both the university's resources and the community.

Each season Summer Lyric Theatre employs the talents of over 200 paid and volunteer actors, singers, dancers, musicians, directors, choreographers, designers and technical artists. Hundreds of individuals audition each year to be a part of the Summer Lyric experience, an experience many characterize as among the best and most professional theatrical opportunities in the Gulf South region. This standard of artistic excellence, for which Summer Lyric is known, has become a hallmark and gives young emergent talent the opportunity to work alongside professionals in a truly collaborative atmosphere.
summary: Summer Lyric Theatre is a musical theatre performing organization based on the campus of Tulane University.

name: Black Excellence in Medicine
description: Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Tulane Black Excellence in Medicine (TBEM) empowers, supports, and increases representation of students in medicine at Tulane. We build community and foster future leaders for a more equitable healthcare field.

name: Scholars of Finance at Tulane University
description:
Scholars of Finance (SOF) is a nonprofit organization dedicated to inspiring character and integrity in the finance industry by developing the next generation of principled leaders. Founded on the belief that finance can be a force for good, SOF equips students with both the technical skills and ethical foundation necessary to lead with purpose.

The organization partners with universities, financial professionals, and firms to provide mentorship, values-based leadership training, and experiential learning opportunities. Its programming emphasizes five core values—integrity, humility, accountability, courage, and curiosity—helping students apply these principles as they pursue careers in finance.

Through speaker series, mentorship programs, leadership symposia, and national conferences, SOF fosters dialogue between students and industry leaders about the role of finance in creating positive societal impact. By connecting ambitious students with experienced professionals, the organization helps bridge the gap between academic preparation and real-world application, instilling a long-term vision of leadership grounded in service and responsibility.

SOF’s mission is not only to prepare future financial leaders for career success, but also to inspire them to drive meaningful change in capital markets and in society at large.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Scholars of Finance (SOF) is a nonprofit that develops future finance leaders through mentorship, values-based training, and community, inspiring integrity, character, and principled leadership in the finance industry.

name: Tulane Blood Drive Association
description:
The goal of this club is to partner with the New Orleans Blood Center to both set up in person volunteer opportunities for members, and to host a five day blood drive on campus every semester.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: The goal of this club is to partner with the New Orleans Blood Center to both set up in person volunteer opportunities for members, and to host a five day blood drive on campus every semester.

name: The Green Wave Stargazers
description:
Green Wave Stargazers is Tulane’s amateur astronomy and astrophotography club, dedicated to exploring the night sky and sharing our passion for space. We host stargazing sessions, astrophotography workshops, and hands-on telescope demos while teaching members about light pollution, celestial navigation, and deep-sky observing. Alongside our educational focus, we also mix in fun social events to build a welcoming community for anyone curious about the cosmos.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Green Wave Stargazers is Tulane’s amateur astronomy and astrophotography club, dedicated to exploring the night sky and sharing our passion for space. We host stargazing sessions, astrophotography workshops, and hands-on telescope demos.

name: Tulane University Arab Student Association
description: TUASA is established for the purpose of introducing, celebrating, teaching, and fostering community in regards to Arab and Arab-American cultural identity, experience, and tradition. TUASA seeks to foster an inclusive community for Arabs, Arab-Americans, and non-Arabs interested in Arab culture, tradition, politics, and history. TUASA will hold general body meetings, celebrations, events, and offer food, guest speakers, and more to its members and guests. Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: TUASA is established for the purpose of introducing, celebrating, teaching, and fostering community in regards to Arab and Arab-American cultural identity, experience, and tradition.

organizationType.name: Recognized Student Organizations - Multicultural, Religious & GSAC

name: Tulane Rocketry
description:
We are an organization aimed at competing in aerospace competitions with other colleges throughout the country. We work with all majors with an interest in an aerospace career. We also do STEM outreach with the NOLA community.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: We are an organization aimed at competing in aerospace competitions with other colleges throughout the country. We work with all majors with an interest in an aerospace career. We also do STEM outreach with the NOLA community.

name: Black Scholars' Circle
description:
Black Scholars’ Circle is established for the purpose of fostering a safe, affirming, and empowering community that centers the academic success, cultural identity, and collective growth of Black students at a predominantly white institution. Through study halls, mentorship, workshops, and community-building events, we seek to uplift Black scholarship and excellence, promote holistic well-being, and cultivate a sense of belonging for every participant who joins our circle.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Fostering a safe, affirming, and empowering community that centers the academic success, cultural identity, and collective growth of Black students at a predominantly white institution.

name: Deming Department of Medicine Section of Nephrology and Hypertension
description: The Nephrology and Hypertension mission is to develop exceptional nephrologists equipped to provide outstanding patient care across diverse populations while fostering academic inquiry, leadership, and innovation in the field of nephrology and hypertension.
summary: Section of Nephrology and Hypertension SoM

name: Colleges Against Cancer at Tulane University
description:
Below I have copied and pasted the Introduction to Colleges Against Cancer from their official website

 

What is Colleges Against Cancer?

Colleges Against Cancer is a program designed to help eliminate cancer by initiating and supporting the programs of the American Cancer Society on college campuses. It is a program designed by and administered by college students from across the country and is supported by the American Cancer Society. The program is designed to allow students and staff to work through many different channels to eliminate cancer. These channels are our strategic directions – advocacy, cancer education programs, Relay For Life, and survivorship. Local chapters work alongside their local offices and national leadership to support the efforts of the American Cancer Society in these directions. Whether it is writing letters to Congressmen, organizing a Relay For Life, or teaching students about preventing cancer – the program enables college students to battle cancer on their campuses.

Mission Statement

American Cancer Society Colleges Against Cancer is a nationwide collaboration of college students, faculty, and staff dedicated to eliminating cancer by initiating and supporting programs of the American Cancer Society in college communities.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.

name: Tulane Artificial Intelligence Society
description:
The Tulane Artificial Intelligence Society is a cross-campus community for learning, building, and debating AI with purpose. All majors and experience levels are welcome. We explore how AI shapes research, industry, and everyday life through beginner-friendly workshops (prompt engineering, agentic AI, media generation), project nights, speaker panels, and conversations about fairness, transparency, and safety. Our core focus spans four pillars: studying AI itself, examining the philosophy of AI (what it is, what it should be, and how it ought to be used), boosting student productivity with practical AI tools, and advancing education with AI to learn more effectively. We connect students with faculty, alumni, and industry mentors to turn curiosity into portfolio-ready projects and career opportunities. TAIS is an engaging, collaborative environment where students learn, grow, and question AI together. Join as a member or step into leadership on committees for Technical Projects, Ethics & Social Impact, Professional Development, or Outreach & Inclusion. Together, we build practical skills, think critically about impact, and create AI solutions for Tulane and New Orleans.

Education & Ethics
TAIS exists to enhance our education. We help students use AI responsibly to improve research, writing, prototyping, and creativity while upholding academic integrity. We follow Tulane’s AI policies and ethical standards: clear disclosure when AI is used, proper citation, privacy-safe practices, and a commitment to fairness and safety. By understanding how AI works—and where it falls short—we become better learners, collaborators, and leaders.

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: Ready to explore AI? TAIS brings together curious minds at every level. Discuss innovations and ethics, master prompting and media generation, and discover tools that will elevate your education and career. The future of AI is now. Join the conversation!

description:
Pads for Pals is a nonprofit organization at Tulane University dedicated to providing basic health necessities in campus bathrooms. Our goal is to install at least one dispenser in each building around campus to prevent missed classes, the stigma around menstruation, and the fear of forgetting menstrual products in dorm rooms while learning. The lack of menstrual products in school bathrooms increases the risk of infection and leads to the use of inappropriate substitutes such as toilet paper and paper towels. Through fundraising events, we hope to expand our project to middle schools in New Orleans, increasing access to menstrual products in school bathrooms. Through this effort, we hope to get rid of the taboo surrounding menstruation to create a more inclusive environment.

organizationType.name: Recognized Student Organizations - Multicultural, Religious & GSAC

name: Tulane Global Health Alliance
description:
Abstract:

TUGHA is a student-run global health organization designed to bridge the gap between interest in global health and meaningful engagement with the field. Rather than functioning as a discussion club or a one-off service org, TUGHA operates as a hub that combines hands-on global health work, career pathways, mentorship, and exposure to the real-world of global health practice.

Each semester, members rank their preference of a variety of timely and relevant global health issues, the selected becoming the club’s term project. TUGHA members engage in research on culturally conscious care, evidence-based interventions, and existing aid models, and work to identify and partner with effective organizations addressing the issue. TUGHA supports this project through fundraising, educational programming, and a culminating showcase of outcomes and impact.

All the while, TUGHA provides ongoing access to global health resources, professional development, and guest speakers to support student learning and exposure to the field.

Ultimately, TUGHA aims to go beyond campus to make a lasting impact worldwide.

Organization Description: 

TUGHA: Tulane Global Health Alliance

A student-led global health hub grounded in real impact, mentorship, and community

The goal is to create something that feels serious but energizing, ambitious but sustainable, and deeply useful to students at different stages.

How TUGHA works (the core structure):

Each semester, TUGHA operates across three interconnected layers, with varying levels of commitment.

  1. Flagship Global Health Project (the spine)

Each semester, the club selects one carefully scoped global health project that students can realistically complete within a semester. This project anchors the organization, giving it focus and credibility.

Projects may include:

  • research or policy briefs for NGOs or research groups
  • health education or communication materials
  • targeted fundraising tied to a specific health intervention
  • program evaluation or needs-assessment support

A small project team (roughly 8–12 students) works weekly on this initiative with clear roles, deadlines, and a final deliverable.

This is where tangible impact and serious experience happen.

  1. Global Health Resources & Career Pathways (the backbone)

Alongside the project, TUGHA functions as a resource and mentorship hub for students interested in global health careers, summer work, and research.

This includes:

  • curated lists of internships, fellowships, summer programs, and research opportunities
  • peer mentorship from upperclassmen and alumni
  • resume, CV, and statement workshops
  • informal panels like “How I got my global health internship”
  1. Education, Exposure & Community (the atmosphere)

To keep the club intellectually exciting and connected to real-world practice, TUGHA hosts:

  • occasional guest speakers (NGO staff, researchers, clinicians, policy professionals)

  • fireside chats on different global health career paths

  • discussions on ethics, health systems, and global inequities

These events will happen very infrequently for maximum impact!

 

What a semester looks like:

Early semester

  • Vision kickoff meeting
  • Presentation of 2–3 realistic project options
  • Student ranking/voting
    Project team formation

Mid-semester

  • Weekly project team meetings
  • Occasional speaker or workshop events
  • Ongoing resource sharing and mentorship

End of semester

  • Completion and delivery of the project
  • Reflection and internal evaluation
  • Showcase Planning for the next semester

This pacing keeps the club focused, exciting, and sustainable.

TUGHA will begin with:

  • one flagship project
  • a small, committed leadership team
  • limited but intentional programming

One year from now, the goal is for members to say:

“This club helped me understand global health in practice, gave me real experience, and opened doors I wouldn’t have known how to access on my own. I made an impact.”

TUGHA is intentionally different from a typical class, service club, or GroupMe you join and forget about. This is your chance to build something, not just take the back seat. Instead of spreading ourselves thin or staying abstract, TUGHA is focused on doing real, well-scoped global health work, while also creating lasting pathways to internships, research, and careers in the field.

Getting involved now means helping shape the foundation. In TUGHA, there is a role for everyone, and all progress is good progress. The founding team won’t just attend meetings. They’ll define the culture, direction, and impact of an alliance that’s meant to last beyond one semester and beyond the scope of  Tulane. By the end of this term, being part of TUGHA could mean pointing to a concrete global health initiative you helped deliver, having clearer pathways into summer opportunities, and being part of a close, motivated leadership team building something genuinely meaningful at Tulane.

 

Participation or membership in this organization is nondiscriminatory and open to all students regardless of demographic identity.
summary: TUGHA is a student-run global health organization that transforms interest into impact through semester-long projects, research, partnerships, fundraising, and professional development, connecting students to real-world global health practice beyond camp
